title: Peptic Ulcer Disease and Related Disorders

items.subtopic: Peptic Ulcer Disease and Related Disorders | PEPTIC ULCER DISEASE
items.title: Diagnostic Approach to Dyspepsia: Why Not All Patients with Burning Epigastric Pain Have Peptic Ulcers
items.content: Despite the classic symptom complex of burning epigastric pain exacerbated by fasting and improved with meals being associated with peptic ulcer disease (PUD), more than 90% of patients presenting with dyspepsia do not have ulcers. This highlights the importance of considering other diagnoses in patients with dyspepsia.
keyPoints: >90% of patients with dyspepsia do not have ulcers
Majority of patients with peptic ulcers may be asymptomatic
Burning epigastric pain is a symptom complex associated with PUD but is not diagnostic
items.reference: Peptic Ulcer Disease and Related Disorders · Peptic Ulcer Disease and Related Disorders | PEPTIC ULCER DISEASE

items.subtopic: Peptic Ulcer Disease and Related Disorders | PEPTIC ULCER DISEASE
items.title: Epidemiology and Economic Impact of Peptic Ulcer Disease in the United States
items.content: Peptic ulcer disease (PUD) is a common condition affecting 4 million individuals annually in the United States, with a lifetime prevalence of approximately 8.4%. It has significant economic implications, costing around $6 billion per year due to healthcare expenses and lost productivity.
keyPoints: 4 million individuals affected by PUD annually (new cases and recurrences)
Lifetime overall prevalence of ~8.4%
Slightly higher prevalence in men
~$6 billion annual burden on direct and indirect health care costs
$3 billion spent on hospitalizations, $2 billion on physician office visits, $1 billion in decreased productivity
items.reference: Peptic Ulcer Disease and Related Disorders · Peptic Ulcer Disease and Related Disorders | PEPTIC ULCER DISEASE

items.title: Parietal Cell Function in Acid Secretion
items.content: The parietal cell is crucial for gastric acid secretion, transforming its tubulovesicle membrane into a dense network of canaliculi upon stimulation to facilitate this process.
keyPoints: Parietal cells are located in the neck or isthmus of the oxyntic gland.
They contain H+, K+-adenosine triphosphatase (ATPase) on their tubulovesicle membrane, crucial for acid secretion.
Acid secretion occurs at the apical canalicular surface and requires high energy from numerous mitochondria (30–40% of total cell volume).
items.reference: Peptic Ulcer Disease and Related Disorders · GASTRIC PHYSIOLOGY (part 1)

items.title: Mucosal Defense Mechanisms in the Gastric Epithelium
items.content: The gastric epithelium is protected by a three-level barrier system to prevent mucosal injury from endogenous and exogenous factors.
keyPoints: First line of defense: mucus-bicarbonate-phospholipid layer, creating a pH gradient from 1-2 at the luminal surface to 6–7 along the epithelial cell surface.
Second level involves surface epithelial cells that produce protective factors like heat shock proteins and trefoil factor family peptides.
Restitution allows epithelial cells to migrate and restore damaged regions, regulated by growth factors such as EGF and TGF-α.
items.reference: Peptic Ulcer Disease and Related Disorders · GASTRIC PHYSIOLOGY (part 1)

items.title: Role of Gastric Microvascular System in Mucosal Repair
items.content: The gastric submucosal microvascular system plays a critical role in mucosal defense and repair by providing bicarbonate, nutrients, and oxygen.
keyPoints: Microvascular system neutralizes acid with HCO3- and supplies necessary micronutrients and oxygen.
Locally produced factors like nitric oxide (NO), hydrogen sulfide, and prostacyclin promote vasodilation for vascular protection.
Growth factors such as FGF and VEGF regulate angiogenesis in the gastric mucosa.
items.reference: Peptic Ulcer Disease and Related Disorders · GASTRIC PHYSIOLOGY (part 1)

items.title: Role of Prostaglandins in Gastric Mucosal Protection
items.content: Prostaglandins are crucial for gastric epithelial defense and repair by regulating mucosal bicarbonate and mucus release, inhibiting parietal cell secretion, maintaining mucosal blood flow, and supporting epithelial cell restitution. They are synthesized from arachidonic acid via the cyclooxygenase (COX) pathway.
keyPoints: Prostaglandins regulate mucosal bicarbonate/mucus release, inhibit parietal cells, maintain mucosal blood flow, support epithelial cell restitution.
Synthesized from arachidonic acid through COX enzymes.
COX-1 is constitutive and maintains GI mucosal integrity; COX-2 is inducible by inflammation.
NSAIDs inhibit both COX-1 (causing GI toxicity) and COX-2 (reducing inflammation).
Selective COX-2 inhibitors reduce inflammation but increase CV risk, leading to FDA withdrawal of valdecoxib and rofecoxib.
items.reference: Peptic Ulcer Disease and Related Disorders · GASTRIC PHYSIOLOGY (part 2)

items.title: Importance of Nitric Oxide in Gastric Mucosal Integrity
items.content: Nitric oxide (NO) is vital for gastric mucosal integrity, produced by constitutively expressed NO synthase. It enhances cytoprotection through stimulation of gastric mucus production, increased mucosal blood flow, and maintenance of epithelial cell barrier function.
keyPoints: NO contributes to cytoprotection in the stomach.
Produced by NO synthase, which is constitutively expressed in the mucosa.
Functions include stimulating gastric mucus, increasing mucosal blood flow, maintaining epithelial cell barrier.
items.reference: Peptic Ulcer Disease and Related Disorders · GASTRIC PHYSIOLOGY (part 2)

items.title: Impact of Helicobacter pylori on Gastric Immunology and Microbiome
items.content: The discovery of H. pylori has highlighted the stomach's complex immunologic system, involving both innate and adaptive immune responses. It also revealed a diverse gastric microbiota that plays a role in various gastric pathologies.
keyPoints: H. pylori triggers an interplay between innate (dendritic cells, epithelial cells, neutrophils, macrophages) and adaptive (B and T cells) immune responses.
Helper T cells are significant in regulating gastric physiology through cytokine production.
The stomach hosts a diverse microbiota, contrary to previous beliefs of it being sterile.
Gastric microbiome's role in health/disease is under investigation but may affect PUD, gastritis, and gastric cancer.
items.reference: Peptic Ulcer Disease and Related Disorders · GASTRIC PHYSIOLOGY (part 2)

items.title: Mechanisms of Gastric Acid Secretion
items.content: Understanding the physiology of gastric acid secretion is crucial for comprehending the pathophysiology of peptic ulcer disease and therapeutic options. Acid secretion occurs under basal and stimulated conditions, with basal secretion influenced by cholinergic and histaminergic inputs, while stimulated secretion involves cephalic, gastric, and intestinal phases.
keyPoints: Basal acid production follows a circadian pattern, highest at night, lowest in the morning.
Cholinergic input via vagus nerve and histaminergic input from local sources contribute to basal secretion.
Stimulated secretion occurs in cephalic (sight, smell, taste), gastric (food entry, nutrient stimulation), and intestinal phases.
Gastrin release is stimulated by nutrients and stomach distention, activating parietal cells via direct and indirect mechanisms.
Somatostatin inhibits acid production directly on parietal cells and indirectly by reducing histamine, ghrelin, and gastrin.
items.reference: Peptic Ulcer Disease and Related Disorders · GASTRIC PHYSIOLOGY (part 3)

items.title: Role of Parietal Cells in Acid Secretion
items.content: Parietal cells are central to gastric acid secretion, located in the oxyntic gland and expressing receptors for stimulants like histamine, gastrin, and acetylcholine. They also secrete intrinsic factor and IL-11.
keyPoints: Parietal cells express $H_2$, cholecystokinin 2/gastrin, and muscarinic $M_3$ receptors.
$H^+$, $K^+$-ATPase enzyme in parietal cells generates high $H^+$ ion concentration using ATP.
Proton pumps are recycled to inactive state in cytoplasmic vesicles post-secretion.
Acid secretion requires chloride and potassium channels in parietal cell membranes.
Sonic hedgehog family proteins may regulate gastric epithelium differentiation and mucosal defense.
items.reference: Peptic Ulcer Disease and Related Disorders · GASTRIC PHYSIOLOGY (part 3)

items.title: Regulation of Gastric Acid Secretion
items.content: Gastric acid secretion is tightly regulated by various hormones and neural inputs. Somatostatin, amylin, ANP, cholecystokinin, ghrelin, IL-11, obstatin, secretin, and serotonin play roles in counterbalancing acid production.
keyPoints: Somatostatin inhibits acid by acting on parietal cells and reducing histamine, ghrelin, and gastrin release.
Neural (central and peripheral) and humoral factors regulate acid secretion.
Ghrelin and motilin may increase gastric acid via histamine release from ECL cells, though this is not confirmed.
items.reference: Peptic Ulcer Disease and Related Disorders · GASTRIC PHYSIOLOGY (part 3)

items.title: Role and Activation of Pepsin in Gastric Acid Environment
items.content: Pepsinogen, secreted by chief cells, is activated to pepsin in the stomach's acidic environment. The optimal pH for pepsin activity is below 2, with significant reduction at pH 4 and inactivation at pH ≥7.
keyPoints: Chief cells secrete pepsinogen
Pepsin activation occurs at pH < 2
Pepsin activity diminishes significantly at pH 4
Pepsin is irreversibly inactivated at pH ≥ 7
items.reference: Peptic Ulcer Disease and Related Disorders · GASTRIC PHYSIOLOGY (part 4) | PATHOPHYSIOLOGIC BASIS OF PUD (part 1)

items.title: Risk Factors and Epidemiology of Peptic Ulcer Disease
items.content: H. pylori and NSAIDs are primary risk factors for PUD, with odds ratios of 3.7 and 3.3 respectively in the US. Other notable risk factors include chronic obstructive lung disease (2.34) and current tobacco use (1.99). The incidence of duodenal ulcers has decreased due to reduced H. pylori prevalence.
keyPoints: H. pylori odds ratio: 3.7
NSAIDs odds ratio: 3.3
Chronic obstructive lung disease odds ratio: 2.34
Current tobacco use odds ratio: 1.99
Duodenal ulcer incidence in Western population: 6–15%
Reduction in duodenal ulcers linked to decreased H. pylori prevalence
items.reference: Peptic Ulcer Disease and Related Disorders · GASTRIC PHYSIOLOGY (part 4) | PATHOPHYSIOLOGIC BASIS OF PUD (part 1)

items.title: Pathophysiology of Duodenal and Gastric Ulcers
items.content: Duodenal ulcers are often associated with increased gastric acid secretion, while gastric ulcers may occur in the presence of normal or decreased acid levels. Both types can be linked to H. pylori or NSAID-induced mucosal damage.
keyPoints: Increased basal and nocturnal gastric acid secretion in duodenal ulcer patients
Decreased bicarbonate secretion in active duodenal ulcers
Gastric ulcers may have normal or decreased acid output
Both ulcer types linked to H. pylori or NSAID-induced damage
items.reference: Peptic Ulcer Disease and Related Disorders · GASTRIC PHYSIOLOGY (part 4) | PATHOPHYSIOLOGIC BASIS OF PUD (part 1)

items.title: Etiology Shift in Peptic Ulcer Disease: H. pylori vs NSAIDs
items.content: While H. pylori was once considered the primary cause of peptic ulcer disease (PUD), recent studies indicate a shift, with only one-fourth of duodenal ulcers (DUs) and one-sixth of gastric ulcers (GUs) being associated with H. pylori infection. This change is attributed to successful eradication efforts and improved sanitary conditions, leading NSAIDs to become the most common cause of PUD.
keyPoints: Only 1/4 of DUs and 1/6 of GUs are associated with H. pylori infection.
NSAIDs have become the most common cause of PUD.
Eradication efforts may reduce gastric cancer in high-risk populations without chronic atrophic gastritis or gastric metaplasia.
items.reference: Peptic Ulcer Disease and Related Disorders · PATHOPHYSIOLOGIC BASIS OF PUD (part 2)

items.title: Pathogenesis of H. pylori and its Role in Gastric Diseases
items.content: H. pylori, a gram-negative microaerophilic rod, resides in the stomach's mucous gel coating or between the mucous layer and gastric epithelium. It does not typically invade cells but can transform into a coccidoid form for survival under adverse conditions. The bacterium's pathogenicity is linked to its genome, which encodes ~1500 proteins including urease, Vac A, and Cag A, facilitating colonization and disease progression.
keyPoints: H. pylori is an S-shaped (~0.5–3 µm) gram-negative microaerophilic rod.
Genome size: 1.65 million base pairs encoding ~1500 proteins.
Key pathogenic factors include urease, Vac A, and Cag A from the cag-PAI.
Cag A translocates into host cells, influencing cell growth and cytokine production.
items.reference: Peptic Ulcer Disease and Related Disorders · PATHOPHYSIOLOGIC BASIS OF PUD (part 2)

items.title: Declining Global Prevalence of H. pylori
items.content: The global prevalence of H. pylori infection in adults has decreased from 50–55% to 43% between 2014 and 2020, influenced by improved socioeconomic status, living standards, hygiene conditions, and increased use of antibiotics for eradication.
keyPoints: Global prevalence of H. pylori in adults declined from 50-55% to 43% (2014-2020)
Contributing factors: improved socioeconomic status, enhanced hygiene, antibiotic use
In developing regions, up to 80% may be infected by age 20; in industrialized countries, prevalence is 20–50%
U.S. prevalence ~30%, higher in individuals born before 1950
10% of Americans <30 years are colonized with H. pylori
items.reference: Peptic Ulcer Disease and Related Disorders · PATHOPHYSIOLOGIC BASIS OF PUD (part 3)

items.title: Transmission and Risk Factors for H. pylori Infection
items.content: H. pylori transmission occurs via oral-oral or fecal-oral routes, with risk declining in developing countries. Higher colonization rates are associated with poor socioeconomic status, less education, domestic crowding, unsanitary conditions, unclean food/water, and exposure to gastric contents of infected individuals.
keyPoints: Transmission: oral-oral or fecal-oral route
Risk declining in developing countries (>50% reduction in U.S. over 30 years)
Higher colonization linked to poor socioeconomic status, less education
Other risk factors: birth/residence in a developing country, domestic crowding, unsanitary conditions
items.reference: Peptic Ulcer Disease and Related Disorders · PATHOPHYSIOLOGIC BASIS OF PUD (part 3)

items.title: Pathophysiology of H. pylori and Peptic Ulcer Disease
items.content: H. pylori infection is associated with chronic active gastritis, but only 10–15% develop peptic ulcers. The pathogenesis involves host and bacterial factors; >90% of duodenal ulcers (DUs) are linked to H. pylori, while it's present in one-sixth of gastric ulcer (GU) cases and one-fourth of DU cases.
keyPoints: H. pylori infection leads to chronic active gastritis
10-15% of infected individuals develop peptic ulcers
>90% of DUs associated with H. pylori; present in 1/6 of GUs, 1/4 of DUs
Pathophysiology involves host and bacterial factors
items.reference: Peptic Ulcer Disease and Related Disorders · PATHOPHYSIOLOGIC BASIS OF PUD (part 3)

items.title: Virulence Factors of H. pylori and Their Role in Pathogenicity
items.content: H. pylori produces various virulence factors that contribute to its pathogenicity, including Cag A, Vac A, and urease. These factors facilitate bacterial residence in the stomach, induce mucosal injury, and modulate host immune responses.
keyPoints: Cag-PAI positive strains are associated with a higher risk of PUD, premalignant gastric lesions, and gastric cancer.
Vac A targets human CD4 T cells, inhibiting their proliferation and disrupting the function of B cells, CD8 T cells, macrophages, and mast cells.
H. pylori-derived urease generates NH3, damaging epithelial cells.
items.reference: Peptic Ulcer Disease and Related Disorders · PATHOPHYSIOLOGIC BASIS OF PUD (part 4)

items.title: Host Immune Response to H. pylori Infection
items.content: The host immune response to H. pylori involves the recruitment of various immune cells and cytokine production, leading to mucosal injury without eradicating the bacteria.
keyPoints: Inflammatory response includes neutrophils, lymphocytes (T and B), macrophages, and plasma cells.
Elevated concentrations of cytokines such as IL-1α/β, IL-2, IL-6, IL-8, TNF α, and IFN γ are found in the gastric epithelium.
H. pylori infection leads to both mucosal and systemic humoral responses.
items.reference: Peptic Ulcer Disease and Related Disorders · PATHOPHYSIOLOGIC BASIS OF PUD (part 4)

items.title: Mechanisms of H. pylori-Induced Epithelial Cell Injury
items.content: H. pylori causes epithelial cell injury through multiple mechanisms, including reactive oxygen/nitrogen species production and apoptosis mediated by interactions with T cells.
keyPoints: Activated neutrophils produce reactive oxygen or nitrogen species contributing to epithelial cell injury.
Apoptosis is related to interaction with TH1 cells and IFN-γ.
H. pylori stimulates NOS2 expression in macrophages and gastric epithelial cells, leading to NO release and cytotoxic effects.
items.reference: Peptic Ulcer Disease and Related Disorders · PATHOPHYSIOLOGIC BASIS OF PUD (part 4)

items.title: Role of H. pylori in Duodenal Ulcer Formation
items.content: H. pylori infection can lead to duodenal ulcer formation through mechanisms such as increased acid production and gastric metaplasia, which allows the bacterium to bind and cause local injury.
keyPoints: Increased basal and stimulated gastrin release in H. pylori-infected individuals
Potential decrease in somatostatin-secreting D cells
H. pylori may increase acid secretion via direct and indirect actions on G, D, and parietal cells
Gastric metaplasia permits H. pylori binding and local injury due to host response
items.reference: Peptic Ulcer Disease and Related Disorders · PATHOPHYSIOLOGIC BASIS OF PUD (part 5)

items.title: Correlation Between Gastritis Type and Ulcer Formation
items.content: The type of gastritis induced by H. pylori correlates with specific gastric pathologies: antral-predominant gastritis is linked to duodenal ulcers, while corpus-predominant gastritis can lead to gastric ulcers and carcinoma.
keyPoints: Antral-predominant gastritis associated with DU formation
Corpus-predominant gastritis predisposes to GUs, gastric atrophy, and gastric carcinoma
items.reference: Peptic Ulcer Disease and Related Disorders · PATHOPHYSIOLOGIC BASIS OF PUD (part 5)

items.title: Epidemiology and Economic Impact of NSAIDs
items.content: Nonsteroidal anti-inflammatory drugs (NSAIDs) are among the most commonly used medications globally, with significant economic implications. Over $7 billion is spent annually on NSAIDs worldwide, and in the United States alone, more than 30 million individuals take these medications, supported by over 100 million prescriptions each year.
keyPoints: NSAIDs account for >$7 billion in annual global spending.
>30 million individuals use NSAIDs in the U.S.
>100 million NSAID prescriptions are sold yearly in the U.S.
COX-2 inhibitors led to over 111 million NSAID prescriptions in 2000, costing $4.8 billion.
items.reference: Peptic Ulcer Disease and Related Disorders · NSAID-INDUCED DISEASE (part 1)

items.title: Clinical Complications and Risk Factors of NSAIDs
items.content: NSAID use is associated with a broad spectrum of gastrointestinal complications, ranging from common dyspepsia to serious conditions like peptic ulceration. Despite the prevalence of these issues, many patients do not experience preceding symptoms, highlighting the importance of identifying those at increased risk.
keyPoints: 50-60% of NSAID users report nausea and dyspepsia.
15-30% develop endoscopy-documented peptic ulceration.
1.5% per year may experience GI complications like bleeding or perforation.
NSAID-induced GI bleeding leads to 60,000–120,000 hospital admissions annually in the U.S.
16,000 deaths per year are related to NSAID toxicity.
4-5% develop symptomatic ulcers within one year of use.
>80% with serious complications did not have preceding dyspepsia.
items.reference: Peptic Ulcer Disease and Related Disorders · NSAID-INDUCED DISEASE (part 1)

items.title: Pathophysiology and Risk Factors for NSAID-Induced Mucosal Injury
items.content: The pathogenesis of NSAID-induced mucosal injury involves the disruption of prostaglandin synthesis, which is crucial for maintaining gastroduodenal mucosal integrity. This process can be exacerbated by factors such as H. pylori infection and genetic predispositions.
keyPoints: Prostaglandins are essential for gastroduodenal mucosal protection.
NSAIDs impair mucosal defense by inhibiting prostaglandin synthesis.
Neutrophil adherence in the gastric microcirculation is key to NSAID-induced injury.
H. pylori infection increases risk of PUD-associated GI bleeding with low-dose aspirin use.
Risk factors include advanced age, history of ulcers, glucocorticoid use, high-dose or multiple NSAIDs, anticoagulants, clopidogrel, and serious diseases.
items.reference: Peptic Ulcer Disease and Related Disorders · NSAID-INDUCED DISEASE (part 1)

items.title: Impact of Smoking on Peptic Ulcer Disease
items.content: Smoking is associated with a higher frequency of peptic ulcers, decreased healing rates, impaired response to therapy, and increased ulcer-related complications such as perforation. The exact mechanism for this increased risk remains unclear.
keyPoints: Smokers have more frequent ulcers than nonsmokers.
Smoking decreases ulcer healing rates and impairs therapeutic response.
Increases the risk of ulcer-related complications like perforation.
Possible mechanisms include altered gastric emptying, decreased bicarbonate production, increased H. pylori infection risk, and generation of mucosal free radicals.
items.reference: Peptic Ulcer Disease and Related Disorders · NSAID-INDUCED DISEASE (part 2)

items.title: Genetic Predisposition in Peptic Ulcer Disease
items.content: Certain genetic factors may predispose individuals to peptic ulcer disease. First-degree relatives of duodenal ulcer patients are three times more likely to develop ulcers, and specific blood groups and genotypes have been implicated.
keyPoints: First-degree relatives of DU patients are three times as likely to develop an ulcer.
Blood group O and nonsecretor status are associated with increased risk for peptic diathesis.
H. pylori preferentially binds to group O antigens, which may influence susceptibility.
Genes encoding NSAID-metabolizing enzymes (CYP2C9 and CYP2C8) are potential susceptibility genes, though studies are inconsistent.
items.reference: Peptic Ulcer Disease and Related Disorders · NSAID-INDUCED DISEASE (part 2)

items.title: Epigastric Pain Patterns and Red Flags in Peptic Ulcer Disease
items.content: In peptic ulcer disease, epigastric pain is a common symptom but has poor predictive value for diagnosing duodenal or gastric ulcers. Typical pain patterns include burning or gnawing discomfort occurring 90 minutes to 3 hours post-meal and relief by antacids or food. Pain awakening patients between midnight and 3 A.M. is more specific to duodenal ulcers but also occurs in non-ulcer dyspepsia. Red flags for complications include constant pain, pain radiating to the back (indicative of penetrating ulcer), sudden severe abdominal pain (suggestive of perforation), and pain worsening with meals or vomiting undigested food (gastric outlet obstruction).
keyPoints: Epigastric pain has poor predictive value for DU/GU.
Two-thirds of PUD patients may not experience abdominal pain.
Midnight to 3 A.M. awakening pain is specific but not exclusive to DU.
Constant pain or pain radiating to the back suggests penetrating ulcer.
Sudden severe abdominal pain indicates possible perforation.
Pain worsening with meals and vomiting undigested food suggest gastric outlet obstruction.
items.reference: Peptic Ulcer Disease and Related Disorders · CLINICAL FEATURES (part 1)

items.title: Physical Examination Findings in Peptic Ulcer Disease
items.content: Epigastric tenderness is the most frequent physical finding in patients with peptic ulcer disease, though its predictive value is low. Important signs of complications include tachycardia and orthostasis (indicative of dehydration from vomiting or GI blood loss), a board-like abdomen suggesting perforation, and a succussion splash indicating gastric outlet obstruction.
keyPoints: Epigastric tenderness is common but not highly predictive.
Tachycardia and orthostasis suggest dehydration due to bleeding or vomiting.
Board-like abdomen indicates possible perforation.
Succussion splash suggests retained fluid, indicating gastric outlet obstruction.
items.reference: Peptic Ulcer Disease and Related Disorders · CLINICAL FEATURES (part 1)

items.title: Complications of Peptic Ulcer Disease: Bleeding and Perforation
items.content: Gastrointestinal bleeding is the most common complication in peptic ulcer disease, occurring in 19.4–57 per 100,000 individuals or about 15% of patients, with a high mortality rate (2.5–10%) often due to non-bleeding causes like multiorgan failure and pulmonary complications. Perforation occurs in 6–7% of PUD cases, with a 30-day mortality rate exceeding 20%. Elderly patients are at higher risk for both bleeding and perforation, likely due to increased NSAID use.
keyPoints: GI bleeding is the most common PUD complication (19.4–57 per 100,000).
Bleeding complications occur in ~15% of PUD patients.
30-day mortality from PUD-related bleeding can be 2.5–10%.
Perforation occurs in 6–7% of PUD cases with >20% 30-day mortality.
Elderly and NSAID users are at higher risk for complications.
items.reference: Peptic Ulcer Disease and Related Disorders · CLINICAL FEATURES (part 1)

items.title: Gastric Outlet Obstruction: Identification and Management
items.content: Gastric outlet obstruction is a rare complication of peptic ulcer disease, occurring in 1–2% of patients. It can be relative or fixed due to inflammation, edema, or scar formation around the pylorus. Symptoms include early satiety, nausea, vomiting, increased postprandial pain, and weight loss.
keyPoints: Occurs in 1-2% of peptic ulcer disease patients
Symptoms: early satiety, nausea, vomiting, increased postprandial abdominal pain, weight loss
Management may require endoscopic or surgical intervention for fixed obstruction
items.reference: Peptic Ulcer Disease and Related Disorders · CLINICAL FEATURES (part 2)

items.title: Differential Diagnosis of Dyspepsia and Ulcer-like Symptoms
items.content: Functional dyspepsia is a common cause of upper abdominal discomfort, with symptoms like postprandial fullness and epigastric burning pain. It can mimic peptic ulcer disease but often has no identifiable organic cause. Other conditions presenting similarly include proximal GI tumors, gastroesophageal reflux, vascular disease, pancreaticobiliary disease, and gastroduodenal Crohn's disease.
keyPoints: Functional dyspepsia occurs in up to 30% of the U.S. population
Up to 80% of patients with dyspepsia have a negative diagnostic evaluation
Differential diagnosis includes proximal GI tumors, gastroesophageal reflux, vascular disease, pancreaticobiliary disease, and gastroduodenal Crohn's disease
items.reference: Peptic Ulcer Disease and Related Disorders · CLINICAL FEATURES (part 2)

items.title: Diagnostic Evaluation for Peptic Ulcer Disease
items.content: Given the poor predictive value of abdominal pain alone, endoscopy is preferred over barium studies for diagnosing gastroduodenal ulcers due to its higher sensitivity and specificity. It allows direct visualization, biopsy, and H. pylori testing. Noninvasive tests for H. pylori include serologic testing, 13C-urea breath test, and fecal antigen test.
keyPoints: Endoscopy is preferred over barium studies for diagnosing ulcers
Endoscopy allows direct visualization, biopsy, and H. pylori testing
Noninvasive tests for H. pylori: serologic testing, 13C-urea breath test, fecal antigen test
items.reference: Peptic Ulcer Disease and Related Disorders · CLINICAL FEATURES (part 2)

items.title: Antacid Use and Potential Side Effects
items.content: Antacids, once the primary therapy for peptic ulcers before understanding histamine's role in acid secretion, are now mainly used for symptomatic relief. Commonly mixed antacids include aluminum hydroxide and magnesium hydroxide to balance side effects such as constipation or loose stools.
keyPoints: Antacids like Maalox and Mylanta combine aluminum and magnesium hydroxide to mitigate individual side effects.
Aluminum hydroxide may cause constipation and phosphate depletion, while magnesium hydroxide can lead to diarrhea.
Magnesium-containing antacids should be avoided in chronic renal failure due to hypermagnesemia risk.
Chronic use of aluminum hydroxide can result in neurotoxicity.
items.reference: Peptic Ulcer Disease and Related Disorders · Peptic Ulcer Disease | ACID-NEUTRALIZING/INHIBITORY DRUGS (part 1)

items.title: H2 Receptor Antagonists: Usage and Monitoring
items.content: H2 receptor antagonists, including cimetidine, ranitidine, famotidine, and nizatidine, inhibit acid secretion effectively. Cimetidine may cause antiandrogenic effects like gynecomastia and impotence with long-term use and can interfere with cytochrome P450 metabolism of certain drugs.
keyPoints: H2 receptor antagonists include cimetidine, ranitidine, famotidine, and nizatidine.
Cimetidine may cause reversible gynecomastia and impotence at high doses over extended periods.
Cimetidine inhibits cytochrome P450, affecting drugs like warfarin, phenytoin, and theophylline.
Famotidine is more potent than cimetidine and can be used once daily for ulcer prevention.
items.reference: Peptic Ulcer Disease and Related Disorders · Peptic Ulcer Disease | ACID-NEUTRALIZING/INHIBITORY DRUGS (part 1)

items.title: Optimal Timing and Administration of PPIs
items.content: Proton pump inhibitors (PPIs) are most effective when administered before meals to maximize their acid inhibitory effects. This is due to the need for proton pumps to be activated by food intake, except for immediate-release formulations like omeprazole.
keyPoints: Administer PPIs before a meal for maximum efficacy (except immediate-release omeprazole).
Maximum acid inhibition occurs between 2 and 6 hours post-administration.
Duration of acid inhibition lasts up to 72–96 hours.
items.reference: Peptic Ulcer Disease and Related Disorders · ACID-NEUTRALIZING/INHIBITORY DRUGS (part 2)

items.title: Rebound Acid Hypersecretion After PPI Discontinuation
items.content: Discontinuing PPIs can lead to rebound gastric acid hypersecretion, particularly in H. pylori-negative individuals. This phenomenon is due to gastrin-induced changes and may last up to 2 months post-discontinuation.
keyPoints: Rebound hypersecretion occurs after short-term (e.g., 2 months) PPI use.
Lasts for up to 2 months after stopping the drug.
Occurs due to gastrin-induced ECL cell changes.
items.reference: Peptic Ulcer Disease and Related Disorders · ACID-NEUTRALIZING/INHIBITORY DRUGS (part 2)

items.title: Potential Drug Interactions with PPIs
items.content: PPIs can interfere with the absorption of certain drugs and may inhibit hepatic cytochrome P450 enzymes, affecting drug metabolism. Caution is advised when using PPIs concomitantly with specific medications.
keyPoints: Interference with absorption of ketoconazole, ampicillin, iron, digoxin.
Rabeprazole, pantoprazole, esomeprazole have minimal cytochrome P450 interactions.
Caution with theophylline, warfarin, diazepam, atazanavir, phenytoin.
items.reference: Peptic Ulcer Disease and Related Disorders · ACID-NEUTRALIZING/INHIBITORY DRUGS (part 2)

items.title: Increased Risk of Infections with Long-term PPI Use
items.content: Long-term use of proton pump inhibitors (PPIs) is associated with a higher incidence of community-acquired pneumonia and Clostridioides difficile-associated disease. A meta-analysis indicated a 74% increased risk for C. difficile infection and a 2.5-fold higher risk of reinfection compared to nonusers.
keyPoints: 74% increased risk of C. difficile infection with PPI use
2.5-fold higher risk of C. difficile reinfection with PPIs
FDA safety alert on the association between C. difficile and PPIs
Consideration for caution in elderly patients at risk
items.reference: Peptic Ulcer Disease and Related Disorders · ACID-NEUTRALIZING/INHIBITORY DRUGS (part 3)

items.title: PPI Use Linked to Collagenous Colitis and Nutrient Deficiencies
items.content: Diarrhea associated with PPI use can lead to collagenous colitis, particularly with lansoprazole. Long-term PPIs are also linked to nutrient deficiencies such as iron, vitamin B12, and magnesium, with a 40% increase in hypomagnesemia.
keyPoints: Hazard ratio of 4.5 for collagenous colitis with PPI use
40% increased risk of hypomagnesemia in PPI users
Potential nutrient deficiencies: iron, vitamin B12, magnesium
Consider monitoring complete blood count and specific levels after prolonged PPI use
items.reference: Peptic Ulcer Disease and Related Disorders · ACID-NEUTRALIZING/INHIBITORY DRUGS (part 3)

items.title: PPI-Clopidogrel Interaction: Clinical Implications
items.content: PPIs may reduce the antiplatelet effect of clopidogrel due to competition for cytochrome P450 (CYP2C19). While evidence is mixed, there's a theoretical advantage of pantoprazole over other PPIs. The FDA recommends reevaluating the need for PPIs in patients on clopidogrel.
keyPoints: PPIs may interfere with clopidogrel metabolism via CYP2C19
Mixed evidence on increased mortality and coronary event readmission rates
Theoretical advantage of pantoprazole over other PPIs (unconfirmed)
FDA advises reevaluation of PPI need in patients on clopidogrel
items.reference: Peptic Ulcer Disease and Related Disorders · ACID-NEUTRALIZING/INHIBITORY DRUGS (part 3)

items.title: Optimizing PPI and Clopidogrel Co-administration
items.content: To minimize drug interaction via cytochrome P450, administer the proton pump inhibitor (PPI) 30 minutes before breakfast and clopidogrel at bedtime.
keyPoints: Administer PPI 30 min before breakfast; clopidogrel at bedtime to reduce competition in cytochrome P450 metabolism.
items.reference: Peptic Ulcer Disease and Related Disorders · ACID-NEUTRALIZING/INHIBITORY DRUGS (part 4)

items.title: Considerations for Long-term PPI Use
items.content: Long-term use of PPIs may be associated with increased cardiac risks, dementia, and kidney injury. Special caution is advised in patients aged ≥65 years due to higher prevalence of chronic diseases.
keyPoints: Increased cardiac risks, dementia, and acute/chronic kidney injury are potential long-term side effects of PPIs.
Patients aged ≥65 have a higher risk for these side effects.
Heightened awareness is needed for inappropriate long-term use; taper gradually over 1–2 weeks if discontinuing.
items.reference: Peptic Ulcer Disease and Related Disorders · ACID-NEUTRALIZING/INHIBITORY DRUGS (part 4)

items.title: Sucralfate: A Multifaceted Cytoprotective Agent for Peptic Ulcer Disease
items.content: Sucralfate is a cytoprotective agent that binds to ulcer sites in the stomach and duodenum, forming a viscous paste. It acts through multiple mechanisms including serving as a barrier, promoting mucosal repair by binding growth factors like EGF, enhancing prostaglandin synthesis, and stimulating mucus and bicarbonate secretion.
keyPoints: Sucralfate binds primarily to sites of active ulceration.
Mechanisms include acting as a physicochemical barrier, promoting trophic action, enhancing prostaglandin synthesis, and stimulating mucosal defense and repair.
Toxicity is rare; constipation occurs in 2–3% of patients.
Avoid in chronic renal insufficiency due to risk of aluminum-induced neurotoxicity.
Standard dosing: 1 g qid.
items.reference: Peptic Ulcer Disease and Related Disorders · CYTOPROTECTIVE AGENTS

items.title: Bismuth-Containing Preparations: Dual Role in Ulcer Healing and H. pylori Eradication
items.content: Bismuth-containing preparations, such as CBS and BSS, are used for their protective effects on the gastric mucosa and efficacy against H. pylori. Their exact mechanism of ulcer healing is unclear but they are integral to anti-H. pylori regimens.
keyPoints: Used in treating PUD and effective against H. pylori.
Adverse effects include black stools, constipation, and darkening of the tongue with short-term use.
Long-term high-dose use may lead to neurotoxicity.
Commonly included in anti-H. pylori regimens.
items.reference: Peptic Ulcer Disease and Related Disorders · CYTOPROTECTIVE AGENTS

items.title: Eradication of H. pylori in PUD reduces ulcer recurrence
items.content: The eradication of H. pylori significantly decreases the recurrence rate of peptic ulcers, especially when compared to cases where the organism is not eliminated.
keyPoints: Documented eradication of H. pylori leads to a decrease in ulcer recurrence to <10–20%.
Without eradication, recurrence rates are 59% for gastric ulcers (GU) and 67% for duodenal ulcers (DU).
Eradication may reduce recurrent ulcer bleeding.
items.reference: Peptic Ulcer Disease and Related Disorders · THERAPY OF H. PYLORI (part 1)

items.title: Indications for H. pylori eradication in various conditions
items.content: H. pylori should be eradicated in patients with documented PUD, and also considered in other specific scenarios to prevent complications.
keyPoints: Eradicate H. pylori in all patients with documented PUD regardless of presentation or NSAID use.
Between 60-90% remission of gastric MALT lymphoma with eradication.
Test-and-treat approach recommended for uninvestigated dyspepsia if local H. pylori incidence >20%.
Eradicate in patients using long-term NSAIDs, especially with prior PUD history.
items.reference: Peptic Ulcer Disease and Related Disorders · THERAPY OF H. PYLORI (part 1)

items.title: Challenges and considerations in H. pylori eradication therapy
items.content: Eradication of H. pylori involves combination therapies due to resistance issues, with specific guidelines for treatment duration and drug selection.
keyPoints: No single agent is effective; combination therapy for 14 days provides the greatest efficacy.
Resistance to clarithromycin and metronidazole impacts eradication strategies.
Shorter courses (7–10 days) are less successful than 14-day regimens.
items.reference: Peptic Ulcer Disease and Related Disorders · THERAPY OF H. PYLORI (part 1)

items.title: Choosing Initial Eradication Regimen for H. pylori
items.content: The choice of an initial eradication regimen for H. pylori should aim for efficacy rates between 85–90%. Factors influencing this decision include antibiotic resistance, prior use, and cost.
keyPoints: Initial eradication rates should be 85–90%
Choice influenced by efficacy, patient tolerance, existing antibiotic resistance, prior antibiotic use, and drug costs
Triple therapy (bismuth, metronidazole, tetracycline) was the first effective regimen
items.reference: Peptic Ulcer Disease and Related Disorders · THERAPY OF H. PYLORI (part 2)

items.title: Managing Antibiotic Resistance in H. pylori Treatment
items.content: Antibiotic resistance is a significant concern, with clarithromycin resistance exceeding 15% warranting the use of bismuth quadruple therapy as first-line treatment.
keyPoints: Clarithromycin resistance >15% suggests using bismuth quadruple therapy
Resistance rates: metronidazole (up to 30% in North America), clarithromycin (13–16% in the US)
Amoxicillin resistance <1%, tetracycline and rifabutin <2%
items.reference: Peptic Ulcer Disease and Related Disorders · THERAPY OF H. PYLORI (part 2)

items.title: Side Effects and Compliance in H. pylori Triple Therapy
items.content: Triple therapy for H. pylori can lead to side effects in 20–30% of patients, with specific concerns like pseudomembranous colitis from amoxicillin.
keyPoints: Up to 20–30% experience side effects on triple therapy
Bismuth: black stools, constipation, tongue darkening
Amoxicillin: <1–2% risk of pseudomembranous colitis
items.reference: Peptic Ulcer Disease and Related Disorders · THERAPY OF H. PYLORI (part 2)

items.title: Approach to Treatment Failure in H. pylori Eradication
items.content: When triple therapy fails to eradicate H. pylori, it is often due to resistant organisms or issues such as inadequate acid suppression and poor adherence. Before switching antibiotics, these factors should be addressed.
keyPoints: Failure of eradication usually indicates resistance.
Inadequate acid suppression and non-adherence are common causes of treatment failure.
Addressing compliance and acid suppression is critical before changing therapy.
items.reference: Peptic Ulcer Disease and Related Disorders · THERAPY OF H. PYLORI (part 3)

items.title: Salvage Therapy Options for Resistant H. pylori
items.content: For patients with resistant H. pylori after initial treatment failure, salvage therapies include combinations like PPI, amoxicillin, and rifabutin (86% cure rate), or levofloxacin-based triple therapy.
keyPoints: PPI, amoxicillin, and rifabutin for 10 days have an 86% cure rate.
Levofloxacin-based triple therapy is a second-line option.
No universally accepted regimen exists after two antibiotic failures.
items.reference: Peptic Ulcer Disease and Related Disorders · THERAPY OF H. PYLORI (part 3)

items.title: Sequential and Concomitant Therapies for H. pylori
items.content: Newer approaches like sequential therapy or concomitant therapy show promise, with eradication rates over 90%. Sequential therapy involves initial amoxicillin and PPI followed by tinidazole/clarithromycin or levofloxacin.
keyPoints: Sequential therapy: 5 days of amoxicillin + PPI, then 5 days of PPI + tinidazole/clarithromycin or levofloxacin.
Concomitant therapy: 5 days of PPI, amoxicillin, levofloxacin, and tinidazole with >90% eradication rates.
Experts recommend considering concomitant therapy over clarithromycin-based triple therapy in the U.S.
items.reference: Peptic Ulcer Disease and Related Disorders · THERAPY OF H. PYLORI (part 3)

items.subtopic: THERAPY OF NSAID-RELATED GASTRIC OR DUODENAL INJURY (part 1)
items.title: Management of Active NSAID-Induced Ulcers
items.content: For active NSAID-induced ulcers, the first step is to stop the injurious agent if possible. If cessation isn't feasible due to severe underlying disease, treatment with acid inhibitory agents like PPIs or H2 blockers is recommended. Only PPIs can heal gastric and duodenal ulcers regardless of whether NSAIDs are discontinued.
keyPoints: Stopping the injurious agent is ideal for treating active NSAID-induced ulcers.
PPIs can heal gastric and duodenal ulcers even if NSAIDs continue.
H2 blockers or PPIs are indicated when NSAID cessation isn't possible.
items.reference: Peptic Ulcer Disease and Related Disorders · THERAPY OF NSAID-RELATED GASTRIC OR DUODENAL INJURY (part 1)

items.subtopic: THERAPY OF NSAID-RELATED GASTRIC OR DUODENAL INJURY (part 1)
items.title: Primary Prevention of NSAID-Induced Ulceration
items.content: Primary prevention involves avoiding NSAIDs, using the lowest dose for the shortest time, selecting theoretically less injurious NSAIDs like naproxen and ibuprofen, or using topical preparations. Concomitant medical therapy with PPIs (e.g., misoprostol 200 µg qid) or high-dose H2 blockers (famotidine 40 mg bid) can prevent ulcers. COX-2 inhibitors like celecoxib and rofecoxib, although initially withdrawn due to CV risks, are now considered with caution.
keyPoints: Avoid NSAIDs when possible; use the lowest dose for the shortest duration.
Naproxen and ibuprofen have a lower likelihood of GI toxicity at standard doses.
PPIs or high-dose H2 blockers can prevent NSAID-induced ulcers.
COX-2 inhibitors require caution, especially with low-dose aspirin.
items.reference: Peptic Ulcer Disease and Related Disorders · THERAPY OF NSAID-RELATED GASTRIC OR DUODENAL INJURY (part 1)

items.subtopic: THERAPY OF NSAID-RELATED GASTRIC OR DUODENAL INJURY (part 2)
items.title: Guidelines for NSAID Use in Patients with GI and CV Risks
items.content: For patients requiring NSAIDs, the choice of agent and need for gastric protection depend on their cardiovascular (CV) and gastrointestinal (GI) risk profiles. Those without CV risks but at high potential for GI toxicity should use selective COX-2 inhibitors with PPI or misoprostol. Patients with moderate GI risk but no cardiac issues can opt for a COX-2 inhibitor alone or nonselective NSAIDs with gastric protection.
keyPoints: Nonselective NSAIDs without gastric protection are suitable for individuals not at CV risk, who do not use aspirin, and have low GI complication risks.
For high potential GI toxicity risk but no CV factors, selective COX-2 inhibitors plus PPI or misoprostol are recommended.
Moderate GI risk patients without cardiac issues can use a COX-2 inhibitor alone or nonselective NSAIDs with PPI/misoprostol.
Patients with CV risks needing low-dose aspirin should consider non-NSAID agents or traditional NSAIDs like naproxen with gastric protection if needed.
items.reference: Peptic Ulcer Disease and Related Disorders · THERAPY OF NSAID-RELATED GASTRIC OR DUODENAL INJURY (part 2)

items.subtopic: THERAPY OF NSAID-RELATED GASTRIC OR DUODENAL INJURY (part 2)
items.title: Challenges and Strategies in Ensuring GI Protection with NSAID Therapy
items.content: Ensuring compliance with GI protective measures during NSAID therapy is challenging due to under-prescribing and patient non-compliance. Combination pills containing NSAIDs and gastroprotective agents are available but lack established clinical benefit over separate medications. Professional education, informatics, and financial incentives can improve adherence to guidelines for preventing NSAID-induced GI complications.
keyPoints: GI protective agent use is difficult due to under-prescribing and patient non-compliance.
Combination pills like double-dose famotidine with ibuprofen or diclofenac with misoprostol are available but lack proven clinical benefit over separate medications.
Interventions including education, informatics, and financial incentives can reduce high-risk NSAID prescribing.
items.reference: Peptic Ulcer Disease and Related Disorders · THERAPY OF NSAID-RELATED GASTRIC OR DUODENAL INJURY (part 2)

items.title: Approach to Dyspepsia in Patients Under 60
items.content: For dyspeptic patients under 60 without alarming signs, a test-and-treat strategy for H. pylori is recommended over empirical acid suppression.
keyPoints: Test-and-treat approach with noninvasive diagnostic tools for H. pylori is preferred today.
Empirical therapeutic trial with acid suppression was the common recommendation previously.
items.reference: Peptic Ulcer Disease and Related Disorders · APPROACH AND THERAPY: SUMMARY (part 1)

items.title: Management of Documented Ulcers
items.content: Once an ulcer is confirmed, determine if H. pylori or NSAID use is involved and treat accordingly with triple/quadruple therapy for 14 days, followed by acid suppression.
keyPoints: Triple or quadruple therapy recommended for 14 days if H. pylori is present.
Continue acid-suppressing drugs (H2 receptor antagonist or PPIs) for a total of 4–6 weeks.
Eradication of H. pylori should be documented 4 weeks after completing antibiotics using stool antigen test or urea breath test.
items.reference: Peptic Ulcer Disease and Related Disorders · APPROACH AND THERAPY: SUMMARY (part 1)

items.title: Refractory Ulcer Management
items.content: If ulcers do not heal within specified time frames, consider factors like NSAID use, smoking cessation, and exclude malignancy or hypersecretory states before escalating treatment.
keyPoints: GU that fails to heal after 12 weeks and DU after 8 weeks should be considered refractory.
Exclude poor compliance, persistent H. pylori infection, NSAID use, and cigarette smoking.
Consider higher doses of PPI (omeprazole 40 mg/d; lansoprazole 30–60 mg/d) for 8 weeks if ulcers remain refractory.
items.reference: Peptic Ulcer Disease and Related Disorders · APPROACH AND THERAPY: SUMMARY (part 1)

items.title: Recognizing and Managing Free Peritoneal Perforation
items.content: Free peritoneal perforation occurs in ~2–3% of DU patients, with NSAID-induced GU perforations being more common. The classic presentation includes sudden severe abdominal pain with peritoneal signs and pneumoperitoneum on imaging, though this only occurs in two-thirds of cases. Elderly, obese, or immunocompromised individuals may not present classically. Up to 10% of patients might not have antecedent ulcer symptoms.
keyPoints: Free peritoneal perforation occurs in ~2–3% of DU patients.
NSAID-induced GU perforations are more common than those from DU.
Classic presentation: sudden severe abdominal pain, peritoneal signs, pneumoperitoneum on imaging (occurs in two-thirds).
Elderly (>70), obese, and immunocompromised may not present classically.
Up to 10% of patients without antecedent ulcer symptoms.
Early suspicion and intervention with nasogastric suction, IV PPI, antibiotics, surgical consultation are crucial.
items.reference: Peptic Ulcer Disease and Related Disorders · APPROACH AND THERAPY: SUMMARY (part 2)

items.title: Managing Gastric Outlet Obstruction from Peptic Ulcers
items.content: Pyloric channel ulcers or DUs can cause gastric outlet obstruction in ~2–3% of patients due to chronic scarring or impaired motility. Patients may present with early satiety, nausea, vomiting undigested food, and weight loss. Initial management includes nasogastric suction, IV hydration/nutrition, and antisecretory agents for 7-10 days. Persistent mechanical obstruction may require endoscopic balloon dilation; surgery is considered if these fail.
keyPoints: Gastric outlet obstruction occurs in ~2–3% of patients with pyloric channel ulcers or DUs.
Caused by chronic scarring or impaired motility (inflammation, edema, pylorospasm).
Symptoms: early satiety, nausea, vomiting undigested food, weight loss.
Initial management: nasogastric suction, IV hydration/nutrition, antisecretory agents for 7-10 days.
Persistent obstruction may require endoscopic balloon dilation.
Surgery considered if other interventions fail.
items.reference: Peptic Ulcer Disease and Related Disorders · APPROACH AND THERAPY: SUMMARY (part 2)

items.title: Choosing Surgical Interventions for Duodenal Ulcers
items.content: Surgical treatment aims to decrease gastric acid secretion. Options include vagotomy with drainage, highly selective vagotomy, and vagotomy with antrectomy. The choice depends on factors like urgency, extent of ulceration, etiology (H. pylori, NSAIDs, malignancy), and surgeon expertise. Highly selective vagotomy is preferred electively unless recurrence rates are high; vagotomy with antrectomy has the lowest recurrence but highest complication rate.
keyPoints: Surgical treatment aims to decrease gastric acid secretion.
Options: vagotomy with drainage (pyloroplasty, gastroduodenostomy, gastrojejunostomy), highly selective vagotomy, vagotomy with antrectomy.
Choice depends on urgency, ulcer extent, etiology (H. pylori, NSAIDs, malignancy), surgeon expertise.
Highly selective vagotomy preferred electively unless high recurrence rates.
Vagotomy with antrectomy has the lowest recurrence rate (~1%) but highest complication rate.
items.reference: Peptic Ulcer Disease and Related Disorders · APPROACH AND THERAPY: SUMMARY (part 2)

items.title: Operative Procedures for Gastric Ulcers Based on Location and Duodenal Ulcer Presence
items.content: The choice of surgical procedure for gastric ulcers (GUs) depends significantly on the ulcer's location and whether a duodenal ulcer (DU) is present. An antrectomy with Billroth I anastomosis is preferred for antral ulcers, while vagotomy is reserved for cases involving DUs. More radical surgeries like subtotal gastrectomy may be necessary for ulcers near the esophagogastric junction.
keyPoints: Antrectomy with Billroth I anastomosis is optimal for antral ulcers.
Vagotomy is performed if a DU is present.
Subtotal gastrectomy may be required for ulcers near the esophagogastric junction.
Ulcer recurrence approaches 30% with less aggressive procedures like Kelling-Madlener.
items.reference: Peptic Ulcer Disease and Related Disorders · APPROACH AND THERAPY: SUMMARY (part 3)

items.title: Surgical Complications and Outcomes in Peptic Ulcer Disease
items.content: The extent of surgical intervention for peptic ulcer disease (PUD) influences the rate of complications. Minimal procedures like highly selective vagotomy have higher recurrence rates but lower morbidity, while more aggressive surgeries reduce recurrence but increase gastrointestinal dysfunction.
keyPoints: Minimal alteration leads to higher ulcer recurrence and less GI disturbance.
Aggressive procedures result in lower recurrence but greater GI dysfunction.
Morbidity for vagotomy and antrectomy is ≤5%, with mortality ~1%.
Highly selective vagotomy has morbidity of 1% and mortality of 0.3%.
items.reference: Peptic Ulcer Disease and Related Disorders · APPROACH AND THERAPY: SUMMARY (part 3)

items.title: Management of Recurrent Ulceration Post-Surgery
items.content: Post-surgical ulcer recurrence is often due to incomplete vagotomy, inadequate drainage, or retained antrum. Diagnosis involves assessing fasting plasma gastrin levels and gastric acid output during sham feeding tests. Medical therapy with H2 blockers heals 70-90% of postoperative ulcers.
keyPoints: Ulcer recurrence risk relates to the surgical procedure performed.
Common causes include incomplete vagotomy, inadequate drainage, retained antrum, or NSAID use.
Sham feeding tests assess vagus nerve integrity by measuring gastric acid output.
H2 blockers heal 70-90% of postoperative ulcers; PPIs may offer better outcomes.
items.reference: Peptic Ulcer Disease and Related Disorders · APPROACH AND THERAPY: SUMMARY (part 3)

items.title: Understanding and Managing Dumping Syndrome
items.content: Dumping syndrome, characterized by vasomotor and GI symptoms, occurs post-vagotomy and drainage surgeries. It has early and late phases with distinct symptomatology and timing. Early dumping is due to rapid gastric emptying into the small intestine, while late dumping results from hypoglycemia.
keyPoints: Early dumping occurs 15–30 min after meals; symptoms include crampy abdominal discomfort, nausea, diarrhea, tachycardia, and syncope.
Late dumping occurs 90 min to 3 h post-meals with vasomotor symptoms due to hypoglycemia from excessive insulin release.
Symptoms are exacerbated by simple carbohydrates and large fluid intake; up to 50% of patients experience it early on.
Dietary modification is primary therapy, involving small meals without simple carbs or liquids during meals.
Medications like acarbose (α-glucosidase inhibitor) and octreotide (somatostatin analogue) are used for refractory cases.
items.reference: Peptic Ulcer Disease and Related Disorders · APPROACH AND THERAPY: SUMMARY (part 4)

items.title: Management of Postvagotomy Diarrhea
items.content: Postvagotomy diarrhea, occurring in up to 10% of patients post-truncal vagotomy, is due to motility disorders and other factors. Treatment includes antidiarrheals like diphenoxylate or loperamide, with cholestyramine for severe cases.
keyPoints: Postvagotomy diarrhea occurs typically 1–2 h after meals, often intermittent but can be severe.
Causes include motility disorder from vagal fiber interruption and decreased nutrient absorption.
Diphenoxylate or loperamide is useful for symptom control; cholestyramine helps in severe cases.
Surgical reversal of a 10-cm jejunum segment may improve bowel frequency.
items.reference: Peptic Ulcer Disease and Related Disorders · APPROACH AND THERAPY: SUMMARY (part 4)

items.title: Identifying and Treating Bile Reflux Gastropathy
items.content: Bile reflux gastropathy presents with abdominal pain, early satiety, nausea, and vomiting. It involves mucosal erythema without significant inflammation. Treatments include prokinetic agents, cholestyramine, sucralfate, and surgical interventions.
keyPoints: Symptoms include abdominal pain, early satiety, nausea, and vomiting with mucosal erythema on examination.
Histology shows minimal inflammation but epithelial cell injury; bile reflux is implicated.
Prokinetic agents, cholestyramine, and sucralfate are used for treatment.
Severe cases may require nuclear scanning or Roux-en-Y gastrojejunostomy to divert pancreaticobiliary secretions.
items.reference: Peptic Ulcer Disease and Related Disorders · APPROACH AND THERAPY: SUMMARY (part 4)

items.title: Management of Nutrient Deficiencies Post-Gastrojejunostomy
items.content: Patients with a Billroth II gastrojejunostomy may experience iron-deficiency anemia and folate deficiency due to impaired absorption or diminished intake. Vitamin D, calcium, and copper deficiencies are common post-partial gastrectomy, leading to osteoporosis, osteomalacia, and peripheral neuropathy.
keyPoints: Iron-deficiency anemia can occur but responds well to oral iron supplementation.
Folate deficiency may develop due to decreased absorption or intake.
Osteomalacia occurs in up to 25% of post-partial gastrectomy patients as a late complication.
Bone fractures are twice as common after gastric surgery compared to controls.
Elevated alkaline phosphatase, reduced serum calcium, bone pain, and pathologic fractures indicate osteomalacia.
Vitamin D and calcium supplementation should be indefinite, especially in females.
Copper deficiency can lead to ataxia, myelopathy, and peripheral neuropathy.
items.reference: Peptic Ulcer Disease and Related Disorders · APPROACH AND THERAPY: SUMMARY (part 5)

items.title: Increased Risk of Gastric Adenocarcinoma Post-Gastrectomy
items.content: The risk of gastric adenocarcinoma in the gastric stump increases significantly 15 years after resection, with a four- to fivefold increase reported 20–25 years post-resection. The pathogenesis may involve alkaline reflux, bacterial proliferation, or hypochlorhydria.
keyPoints: Incidence of adenocarcinoma in the gastric stump increases 15 years after resection.
Four- to fivefold increase in gastric cancer reported 20–25 years post-resection.
Pathogenesis may involve alkaline reflux, bacterial proliferation, or hypochlorhydria.
Endoscopic screening's role is unclear; most guidelines do not support its use.
items.reference: Peptic Ulcer Disease and Related Disorders · APPROACH AND THERAPY: SUMMARY (part 5)

items.title: Epidemiology and Delay in Diagnosis
items.content: Zollinger-Ellison Syndrome (ZES) is rare, with an incidence of 0.1 to 3% among those presenting with peptic ulcer disease (PUD), translating to 0.5–3 cases per million population annually. Females are slightly more affected than males, and diagnosis typically occurs between ages 30 and 65, often delayed by 4-7 years after symptom onset due to the use of proton pump inhibitors (PPIs) leading to decreased referrals for gastrinoma evaluation.
keyPoints: Incidence: 0.1 to 3% in PUD patients
Annual incidence: 0.5–3 per million population
Females slightly more affected than males
Diagnosis age range: 30-65 years
Delay in diagnosis: 4-7 years after symptoms, can be ≥6 years due to PPI use
items.reference: Peptic Ulcer Disease and Related Disorders · ZOLLINGER-ELLISON SYNDROME (part 1)

items.title: Pathophysiology of ZES
items.content: The pathophysiology of Zollinger-Ellison Syndrome involves hypergastrinemia due to an autonomous neoplasm, leading to excessive gastric acid secretion. Gastrin stimulates acid production by acting on parietal cells and inducing histamine release from ECL cells, causing peptic ulcer diathesis, erosive esophagitis, and diarrhea.
keyPoints: Hypergastrinemia due to autonomous neoplasm
Gastrin stimulates acid secretion via parietal cells and histamine release from ECL cells
Clinical manifestations: Peptic ulcer diathesis, erosive esophagitis, diarrhea
items.reference: Peptic Ulcer Disease and Related Disorders · ZOLLINGER-ELLISON SYNDROME (part 1)

items.title: Clinical Manifestations of ZES
items.content: The primary clinical manifestation of Zollinger-Ellison Syndrome is abdominal pain due to gastric acid hypersecretion. Over 90% of patients present with peptic ulcers, often in unusual locations or refractory to standard therapy. Esophageal symptoms occur in up to two-thirds of cases, and diarrhea affects up to 70%, potentially resolving with PPI therapy.
keyPoints: Abdominal pain due to gastric acid hypersecretion
>90% present with peptic ulcers
Ulcer locations: Unusual or refractory to standard therapy
Esophageal symptoms in up to two-thirds of patients
Diarrhea in up to 70%, may resolve with PPIs
items.reference: Peptic Ulcer Disease and Related Disorders · ZOLLINGER-ELLISON SYNDROME (part 1)

items.title: Importance of MEN1 Gene Mutation in Gastrinomas
items.content: Gastrinomas are associated with MEN 1 syndrome in approximately 25% of patients. The syndrome involves mutations in the MEN1 gene, which encodes menin, a protein crucial for DNA replication and transcriptional regulation.
keyPoints: MEN 1 syndrome is autosomal dominant affecting parathyroid glands (80–90%), pancreas (40–80%), and pituitary gland (30–60%).
Inactivating mutations of the MEN1 tumor suppressor gene on chromosome 11q13 cause the syndrome.
Genetic diagnosis via MEN1 gene sequencing reveals mutations in 70–90% of typical cases.
Clinical diagnosis is necessary when genetic testing fails, based on tumors in two endocrine organs or family history with one organ affected.
items.reference: Peptic Ulcer Disease and Related Disorders · ZOLLINGER-ELLISON SYNDROME (part 2)

items.title: Diagnostic Approach and Management of ZES
items.content: Early diagnosis of Zollinger-Ellison Syndrome (ZES) is crucial to minimize complications from gastric acid hypersecretion. Biochemical measurements, including fasting gastrin levels, are essential in diagnosing ZES.
keyPoints: Establishing MEN 1 diagnosis is critical for genetic counseling and determining surgical approach.
Screening involves detailed family history and serum markers: calcium, parathyroid, prolactin, pancreatic polypeptide.
PPIs complicate diagnostic evaluation by lowering acid secretion and elevating fasting gastrin levels in normal individuals.
Stopping PPIs in ZES patients requires a systematic approach due to significant morbidity risk from peptic diathesis.
items.reference: Peptic Ulcer Disease and Related Disorders · ZOLLINGER-ELLISON SYNDROME (part 2)

items.subtopic: TABLE 335-8 When to Obtain a Fasting Serum Gastrin Level (part 1)
items.title: Indications and Interpretation of Fasting Serum Gastrin Levels
items.content: Fasting serum gastrin levels are crucial in diagnosing Zollinger-Ellison Syndrome (ZES). A level <150 pg/mL, especially if confirmed on two separate occasions while the patient is on a PPI, virtually excludes ZES. Conversely, most gastrinoma patients exhibit levels >150–200 pg/mL.
keyPoints: Normal fasting gastrin: <150 pg/mL
Gastrinoma suspicion: >150–200 pg/mL
PPI use requires stopping for 7 days before testing to avoid false results
items.reference: Peptic Ulcer Disease and Related Disorders · TABLE 335-8 When to Obtain a Fasting Serum Gastrin Level (part 1)

items.subtopic: TABLE 335-8 When to Obtain a Fasting Serum Gastrin Level (part 1)
items.title: Factors Leading to Elevated Fasting Gastrin Levels
items.content: Elevated fasting gastrin levels can result from various conditions including gastric hypochlorhydria, PPI use, and H. pylori infection. Other causes include retained gastric antrum, G-cell hyperplasia, and systemic diseases like rheumatoid arthritis.
keyPoints: Common causes: Gastric hypochlorhydria, PPI use
Overlap with ZES levels possible in common disorders
PPI cessation required for accurate measurement
items.reference: Peptic Ulcer Disease and Related Disorders · TABLE 335-8 When to Obtain a Fasting Serum Gastrin Level (part 1)

items.subtopic: TABLE 335-8 When to Obtain a Fasting Serum Gastrin Level (part 1)
items.title: Secretin Stimulation Test for Diagnosing ZES
items.content: The secretin stimulation test is the most sensitive and specific method for diagnosing gastrinoma. An increase in gastrin levels of ≥120 pg/mL within 15 minutes post-secretin injection indicates ZES with >90% sensitivity and specificity.
keyPoints: Secretin test: Increase in gastrin ≥120 pg/mL
Sensitivity and specificity: >90%
PPI must be stopped for 1 week before testing
items.reference: Peptic Ulcer Disease and Related Disorders · TABLE 335-8 When to Obtain a Fasting Serum Gastrin Level (part 1)

items.subtopic: TABLE 335-8 When to Obtain a Fasting Serum Gastrin Level (part 2)
items.title: Diagnosis and Localization of Gastrinoma
items.content: Gastrinoma diagnosis is confirmed biochemically by elevated gastrin levels and low gastric pH, with tumor localization using imaging studies. Imaging modalities include endoscopic ultrasound (EUS), OctreoScan, PET-CT with $^{68}$Ga-DOTATATE, and $^{18}$F-FDG PET.
keyPoints: Biochemical diagnosis involves elevated gastrin levels and low gastric pH.
Endoscopic ultrasound (EUS) has a sensitivity of 43% for duodenal lesions but is useful for pancreatic imaging (<5 mm resolution).
$^{111}$In-pentetreotide (OctreoScan) shows >80% sensitivity and specificity.
PET-CT with $^{68}$Ga-DOTATATE offers >90% sensitivity and specificity, preferred when available.
Up to 50% of patients have metastatic disease at diagnosis.
items.reference: Peptic Ulcer Disease and Related Disorders · TABLE 335-8 When to Obtain a Fasting Serum Gastrin Level (part 2)

items.subtopic: TABLE 335-8 When to Obtain a Fasting Serum Gastrin Level (part 2)
items.title: Imaging Strategy for Gastrinoma
items.content: After biochemical confirmation, imaging is crucial for excluding metastatic disease and localizing the primary tumor. Preferred studies include abdominal CT scan, MRI, OctreoScan, or PET-CT with $^{68}$Ga-DOTATATE.
keyPoints: Abdominal CT scan, MRI, or PET-CT with $^{68}$Ga-DOTATATE are used to exclude metastatic disease post-biochemical diagnosis.
EUS is useful for pancreatic imaging and assessing lymph nodes/vascular involvement.
PET-CT with $^{68}$Ga-DOTATATE is superior for well-differentiated NETs like gastrinomas.
Surgical approach includes exploratory laparotomy or EUS/endoscopic duodenal exploration.
items.reference: Peptic Ulcer Disease and Related Disorders · TABLE 335-8 When to Obtain a Fasting Serum Gastrin Level (part 2)

items.title: Optimal Initial PPI Dosing in Zollinger-Ellison Syndrome
items.content: Proton pump inhibitors (PPIs) are the primary treatment for managing hormone overproduction symptoms in Zollinger-Ellison Syndrome. The initial dosing of PPIs like omeprazole, lansoprazole, rabeprazole, or esomeprazole should be 60 mg divided doses within a 24-hour period.
keyPoints: PPIs are the treatment of choice for Zollinger-Ellison Syndrome.
Initial PPI dose: 60 mg in divided doses over 24 hours.
Dosing adjusted based on gastric acid analysis to achieve BAO <10 meq/h or <5 meq/h post-surgery.
items.reference: Peptic Ulcer Disease and Related Disorders · Zollinger-Ellison Syndrome (part 1)

items.title: Surgical Outcomes in Sporadic vs. MEN 1 Gastrinoma Patients
items.content: Surgery can be curative for sporadic gastrinoma patients, with immediate cure rates up to 33% and disease-free intervals as high as 95% over ten years. However, surgery is less effective for MEN 1 patients, with fewer than 5% being disease-free five years post-operation.
keyPoints: Immediate cure rate for sporadic gastrinoma: up to 33%.
Disease-free interval in sporadic gastrinoma: up to 95% over ten years.
<5% of MEN 1 patients are disease-free after 5 years post-surgery.
items.reference: Peptic Ulcer Disease and Related Disorders · Zollinger-Ellison Syndrome (part 1)

items.title: Management Strategies for Metastatic Gastrinomas
items.content: For metastatic gastrinomas, systemic tumor-targeted therapy is often deferred until there's evidence of progression or refractory symptoms. Temozolomide with capecitabine shows radiographic regression and progression-free survival in 70% of patients for up to 18 months.
keyPoints: Systemic therapy initiated upon tumor progression or uncontrolled symptoms.
Temozolomide with capecitabine: Radiographic regression in 70%, progression-free survival up to 18 months.
items.reference: Peptic Ulcer Disease and Related Disorders · Zollinger-Ellison Syndrome (part 1)

items.title: Survival Rates and Prognostic Indicators in Zollinger-Ellison Syndrome
items.content: The survival rates for patients with gastrinoma vary significantly based on the extent of tumor resection and presence of metastasis. Complete tumor resection or negative laparotomy results in high 5- and 10-year survival rates (>90%). In contrast, incomplete resections and hepatic metastases are associated with much lower survival rates.
keyPoints: 5- and 10-year survival rates for gastrinoma patients: 62–75% and 47–53%, respectively.
Survival >90% if entire tumor resected or negative laparotomy.
<20% 5-year survival with hepatic metastasis.
Favorable prognostic indicators include primary duodenal wall tumors, isolated lymph node tumor, presence of MEN 1, and undetectable tumor upon surgical exploration.

Poor outcomes associated with higher gastrin levels (>10,000 pg/mL), poor histologic differentiation, high proliferative index, certain molecular changes (chromosome 1qLOH, chromosome XLOH), large pancreatic primary tumors (>2–3 cm), metastatic disease to lymph nodes, liver, and bone, Cushing's syndrome.
items.reference: Peptic Ulcer Disease and Related Disorders · Zollinger-Ellison Syndrome (part 2) | STRESS-RELATED MUCOSAL INJURY

items.title: Pathogenesis and Management of Stress-Related Mucosal Injury
items.content: Stress-related mucosal injury, including stress-induced gastritis or ulcers, occurs in patients with severe physiological stress such as shock or trauma. The pathogenesis involves factors like mucosal ischemia and systemic cytokine release rather than inflammation or H. pylori infection.
keyPoints: Occurs in shock, sepsis, massive burns, severe trauma, head injury.
Most common presentation is GI bleeding (48–72 h post-injury).
Histology shows no inflammation or H. pylori; 'gastritis' is a misnomer.
Factors include mucosal ischemia, systemic cytokines, poor GI motility, oxidative stress.
Prophylaxis with PPIs reduces incidence of bleeding from 20–30% to <5%.
items.reference: Peptic Ulcer Disease and Related Disorders · Zollinger-Ellison Syndrome (part 2) | STRESS-RELATED MUCOSAL INJURY

items.title: Prophylactic Therapy for Stress Ulceration in High-Risk Patients
items.content: In high-risk patients, such as those mechanically ventilated or with coagulopathy, prophylactic therapy with PPIs is recommended to prevent stress ulcer-related GI bleeding. PPIs are preferred over H2 blockers due to their superior efficacy.
keyPoints: High mortality (>40%) associated with clinically important GI bleeding from stress ulcers.
PPIs are the treatment of choice for stress prophylaxis.
Oral PPI is best if enteral administration is possible; intravenous pantoprazole for others.
Meta-analysis shows superiority of PPIs over H2 blockers without increased infection risk or mortality.
items.reference: Peptic Ulcer Disease and Related Disorders · Zollinger-Ellison Syndrome (part 2) | STRESS-RELATED MUCOSAL INJURY

items.title: Understanding Gastritis: Histological Confirmation is Key
items.content: Gastritis should be defined by histologically documented inflammation of the gastric mucosa, not merely endoscopic findings or symptoms like dyspepsia. The clinical presentation does not typically correlate with histologic findings.
keyPoints: Histological documentation is required for diagnosing gastritis.
Endoscopic erythema and dyspepsia are not synonymous with gastritis.
No typical clinical manifestation correlates directly with histologic findings.
items.reference: Peptic Ulcer Disease and Related Disorders · GASTRITIS

items.title: Acute Gastritis: Recognizing Causes and Progression
items.content: Acute gastritis is commonly caused by infectious agents, particularly $H. pylori$. It presents with sudden epigastric pain, nausea, vomiting, and histologic findings of neutrophil infiltrates, edema, and hyperemia. Untreated acute $H. pylori$ infection can progress to chronic gastritis.
keyPoints: Acute gastritis is often due to infectious causes like $H. pylori$.
Symptoms include sudden epigastric pain, nausea, vomiting.
Histology shows neutrophil infiltrates, edema, hyperemia.
Can progress to chronic gastritis if untreated.
May lead to hypochlorhydria lasting up to 1 year.
items.reference: Peptic Ulcer Disease and Related Disorders · GASTRITIS

items.title: Understanding the Pathogenesis and Clinical Implications of Type A Gastritis
items.content: Type A gastritis, primarily affecting the fundus and body with antral sparing, is often associated with autoimmune mechanisms. It is linked to pernicious anemia due to circulating antibodies against parietal cells and intrinsic factor (IF). The presence of H. pylori can mimic this distribution through molecular mimicry.
keyPoints: Type A gastritis involves the fundus and body, sparing the antrum.
>90% of patients with pernicious anemia have antibodies against parietal cells.
Up to 50% of patients with type A gastritis show these antibodies.
Antibodies are directed against $H^+, K^+-$ATPase.
~40% of patients with pernicious anemia have anti-IF antibodies, which are more specific for Type A gastritis.
Higher incidence of HLA-B8 and HLA-DR3 haplotypes suggests autoimmune origin.
Low pepsinogen levels can be used as a diagnostic marker.
items.reference: Peptic Ulcer Disease and Related Disorders · TABLE 335-10 Classification of Gastritis (part 1)

items.title: Recognizing the Progression and Risk Factors in Chronic Gastritis
items.content: Chronic gastritis progresses from superficial to atrophic, then to gastric atrophy. It is classified by histologic characteristics and site involvement. Type A is autoimmune and body-predominant; Type B is H. pylori-related and antral-predominant.
keyPoints: Histological progression: Superficial gastritis → Atrophic gastritis → Gastric atrophy.
Inflammation initially involves superficial and glandular portions of the gastric mucosa.
Type A (autoimmune) affects primarily the fundus and body; Type B (H. pylori-related) is antral-predominant.
Intestinal metaplasia, a risk factor for gastric cancer, can occur in chronic gastritis.
items.reference: Peptic Ulcer Disease and Related Disorders · TABLE 335-10 Classification of Gastritis (part 1)

items.title: Identifying Infectious Causes and Their Clinical Implications
items.content: Acute infectious gastritides include bacterial, viral, parasitic, and fungal infections. H. pylori is a common cause, but other organisms like streptococci and E. coli can also be involved. Immunocompromised patients may develop herpetic or CMV gastritis.
keyPoints: Acute infectious gastritides can be caused by bacteria (e.g., H. pylori), viruses, parasites, fungi.
Organisms include streptococci, staphylococci, E. coli, Proteus, Haemophilus species.
Immunocompromised individuals are at risk for herpetic or CMV gastritis.
Histologic finding of intranuclear inclusions is indicative of CMV gastritis.
items.reference: Peptic Ulcer Disease and Related Disorders · TABLE 335-10 Classification of Gastritis (part 1)

items.title: Achlorhydria and Hypergastrinemia in Pernicious Anemia
items.content: In pernicious anemia, parietal cell destruction leads to achlorhydria. This results in hypergastrinemia due to lack of gastric acid feedback inhibition on G cells.
keyPoints: Achlorhydria results from the loss of parietal cells, which produce intrinsic factor (IF).
Lack of IF leads to vitamin B12 deficiency and its sequelae: megaloblastic anemia and neurologic dysfunction.
Hypergastrinemia occurs due to relative sparing of G cells in the antrum; gastrin levels can exceed 500 pg/mL in pernicious anemia.
ECL cell hyperplasia and gastric carcinoid tumors may develop from chronic hypergastrinemia.
items.reference: Peptic Ulcer Disease and Related Disorders · TABLE 335-10 Classification of Gastritis (part 2)

items.title: H. pylori Infection and Type B Gastritis
items.content: Type B gastritis, caused by H. pylori infection, is antral-predominant but can progress to pangastritis over time.
keyPoints: Type B gastritis is the more common form of chronic gastritis and increases with age, affecting up to 100% of individuals >70 years old.
H. pylori infection leads to a dense inflammatory infiltrate in the antrum initially, which can progress to multifocal atrophic gastritis and metaplasia.
Eradication of H. pylori improves histology and reduces inflammation.
items.reference: Peptic Ulcer Disease and Related Disorders · TABLE 335-10 Classification of Gastritis (part 2)

items.title: H. pylori Infection as a Risk Factor for Gastric Cancer
items.content: Chronic H. pylori infection is an independent risk factor for gastric adenocarcinoma and MALT lymphoma.
keyPoints: H. pylori infection increases the risk of gastric cancer by three- to sixfold, potentially ninefold after adjusting for serologic testing inaccuracies in the elderly.
Eradication of H. pylori is being evaluated as a preventative measure against gastric cancer but not yet recommended.
H. pylori-associated MALT lymphoma may regress with infection eradication; follow-up with EUS every 2–3 months is advised.
items.reference: Peptic Ulcer Disease and Related Disorders · TABLE 335-10 Classification of Gastritis (part 2)

items.title: Management of Atrophic Gastritis with Intestinal Metaplasia
items.content: Patients with atrophic gastritis complicated by intestinal metaplasia, but without dysplasia, should undergo surveillance endoscopy every 3 years.
keyPoints: Surveillance endoscopy recommended every 3 years for atrophic gastritis with intestinal metaplasia
No dysplasia present
items.reference: Peptic Ulcer Disease and Related Disorders · Chronic Gastritis

items.title: Role of H. pylori Eradication in Chronic Gastritis
items.content: Eradication of H. pylori is often recommended even if peptic ulcer disease (PUD) or low-grade MALT lymphoma is not present, as part of managing chronic gastritis.
keyPoints: H. pylori eradication recommended in chronic gastritis management
Even in absence of PUD or low-grade MALT lymphoma
items.reference: Peptic Ulcer Disease and Related Disorders · Chronic Gastritis

items.title: Diagnosis and Treatment of Russell Body Gastritis (RBG)
items.content: Russell body gastritis, characterized by numerous plasma cells with Russell bodies, can be associated with H. pylori infection. It may resolve upon eradication of H. pylori.
keyPoints: Russell body gastritis defined by plasma cells containing Russell bodies
Associated with H. pylori in 7 out of 10 reported cases
Resolution possible with H. pylori eradication
items.reference: Peptic Ulcer Disease and Related Disorders · Chronic Gastritis

items.title: Diagnosis of Ménétrier's Disease
items.content: Ménétrier's disease (MD) is diagnosed through endoscopy with deep mucosal biopsy, often requiring a full-thickness biopsy to exclude other conditions. Initial evaluation includes CBC, serum gastrin, albumin levels, CMV and H. pylori serology, and pH testing of gastric aspirate.
keyPoints: MD presents with large, tortuous mucosal folds, predominantly in the body and fundus.
