Differential diagnosis includes ZES, malignancy, infectious etiologies, gastritis polyposa profunda, and infiltrative disorders like sarcoidosis.
Histology shows massive foveolar hyperplasia and reduced parietal and chief cells.
Initial evaluation: CBC, serum gastrin, albumin levels, CMV/H. pylori serology, pH testing.
items.reference: Peptic Ulcer Disease and Related Disorders · MÉNÉTRIER'S DISEASE | Ménétrier's Disease

items.title: Clinical Presentation and Complications of Ménétrier's Disease
items.content: MD typically presents insidiously with symptoms like epigastric pain, nausea, vomiting, anorexia, peripheral edema, weight loss, and occasional occult GI bleeding. Protein-losing gastropathy occurs in 20 to 100% of patients, leading to hypoalbuminemia and edema.
keyPoints: Symptoms: epigastric pain, nausea, vomiting, anorexia, peripheral edema, weight loss.
Occult GI bleeding is possible; overt bleeding due to superficial mucosal erosions is rare.
Protein-losing gastropathy occurs in 20 to 100% of patients, causing hypoalbuminemia and edema.
items.reference: Peptic Ulcer Disease and Related Disorders · MÉNÉTRIER'S DISEASE | Ménétrier's Disease

items.title: Treatment Approach for Ménétrier's Disease
items.content: Cetuximab, an EGF inhibitory antibody targeting the EGFR pathway overstimulation in MD, is considered first-line treatment. It has shown near-complete histologic remission and symptom improvement in trials. Severe cases with persistent protein loss may require partial or total gastrectomy.
keyPoints: Cetuximab targets EGFR pathway overstimulation, showing promising results.
Four of seven patients on cetuximab had near-complete histologic remission and symptom improvement.
Partial or total gastrectomy is reserved for severe cases with persistent protein loss.
items.reference: Peptic Ulcer Disease and Related Disorders · MÉNÉTRIER'S DISEASE | Ménétrier's Disease

The majority of patients with peptic ulcers are symptomatic.
Peptic ulcers can occur in both the stomach and duodenum.
The lifetime prevalence of PUD in the United States is approximately 15%.

items.explanation: COX-1 is expressed constitutively and plays an important role in maintaining the integrity of gastrointestinal (GI) mucosal.

items.question: What adverse effect has been associated with selective COX-2 inhibitors?

items.question: What is the role of nitric oxide in gastric mucosal integrity?
options: Stimulation of gastric mucus and increased mucosal blood flow
items.explanation: Nitric oxide contributes to cytoprotection by stimulating gastric mucus, increasing mucosal blood flow, and maintaining epithelial cell barrier function.
items.reference: Peptic Ulcer Disease and Related Disorders · GASTRIC PHYSIOLOGY (part 2)

items.question: Which hormone is released from endocrine cells found in the gastric mucosa in response to hydrochloric acid (HCl)?
items.explanation: Somatostatin is released from D cells in the gastric mucosa in response to HCl.

items.question: What enzyme is responsible for generating a high concentration of hydrogen ions ($H^{+}$) in parietal cells?
items.explanation: The $H^{+}, K^{+}$-ATPase enzyme is responsible for generating a high concentration of hydrogen ions in parietal cells.
items.reference: Peptic Ulcer Disease and Related Disorders · GASTRIC PHYSIOLOGY (part 3)

items.question: Which phase of gastric acid secretion is activated once food enters the stomach?
items.explanation: The gastric phase is activated when food enters the stomach.

items.explanation: Gastrin stimulates the cholecystokinin 2/gastrin receptor on parietal cells.

items.question: Where are chief cells, which secrete pepsinogen, primarily located?

items.reference: Peptic Ulcer Disease and Related Disorders · GASTRIC PHYSIOLOGY (part 4) | PATHOPHYSIOLOGIC BASIS OF PUD (part 1)

items.question: What is a common risk factor for peptic ulcer disease with an estimated odds ratio of 3.7 in the United States?

items.question: In which part of the duodenum do most duodenal ulcers occur?
items.explanation: Most duodenal ulcers occur in the first portion of the duodenum.
items.reference: Peptic Ulcer Disease and Related Disorders · GASTRIC PHYSIOLOGY (part 4) | PATHOPHYSIOLOGIC BASIS OF PUD (part 1)

items.question: Which factor is essential for H. pylori's initial infection step in the stomach?
options: Cag A translocation into host cells
items.explanation: The first step in infection by H. pylori is dependent on the bacteria's motility and its ability to produce urease.

items.question: What role does the cag pathogenicity island (cag-PAI) play in H. pylori infection?
options: It encodes proteins that alkalinize the stomach environment.
It translocates Cag A into host cells, affecting cell growth and cytokine production.
It is responsible for the bacterium's motility.
It facilitates bacterial invasion of gastric epithelial cells.
items.explanation: The cag-PAI encodes components of a type IV secretion system that translocates Cag A into host cells, affecting cell growth and cytokine production.
items.reference: Peptic Ulcer Disease and Related Disorders · PATHOPHYSIOLOGIC BASIS OF PUD (part 2)

items.question: Which statement best describes the role of urease in H. pylori infection?
options: Urease helps H. pylori attach to gastric epithelium.
Urease produces ammonia from urea, alkalinizing the surrounding pH.
Urease is involved in bacterial invasion of cells.
Urease translocates Cag A into host cells.
items.explanation: Urease produces ammonia from urea, which is essential for alkalinizing the surrounding pH, aiding H. pylori survival in the stomach.
items.reference: Peptic Ulcer Disease and Related Disorders · PATHOPHYSIOLOGIC BASIS OF PUD (part 2)

items.question: What factor has contributed to the decline in global prevalence of H. pylori infection from 2014 to 2020?
options: Increased use of antibiotics for eradication therapies
Reduced incidence of peptic ulcer disease
items.explanation: The increased use of antibiotics specifically with eradication therapies is a contributing factor to the decline in H. pylori prevalence.
items.reference: Peptic Ulcer Disease and Related Disorders · PATHOPHYSIOLOGIC BASIS OF PUD (part 3)

items.question: Which age group in the United States has the highest rate of H. pylori infection?
items.explanation: Individuals born before 1950 have a higher rate of H. pylori infection than those born later.
items.reference: Peptic Ulcer Disease and Related Disorders · PATHOPHYSIOLOGIC BASIS OF PUD (part 3)

items.question: What is the primary reason for the increased prevalence of H. pylori with age?
items.explanation: The steady increase in prevalence with age is due primarily to a cohort effect, reflecting higher transmission during earlier periods.
items.reference: Peptic Ulcer Disease and Related Disorders · PATHOPHYSIOLOGIC BASIS OF PUD (part 3)

items.question: What are the main factors predisposing individuals to higher H. pylori colonization rates?
options: Poor socioeconomic status and less education about the organism
items.explanation: Factors such as poor socioeconomic status and less education are responsible for higher colonization rates, not race.
items.reference: Peptic Ulcer Disease and Related Disorders · PATHOPHYSIOLOGIC BASIS OF PUD (part 3)

items.question: Which of the following H. pylori virulence factors is encoded within the pathogenicity island (cag-PAI)?
items.explanation: The cag-PAI encodes Cag A and pic B.
items.reference: Peptic Ulcer Disease and Related Disorders · PATHOPHYSIOLOGIC BASIS OF PUD (part 4)

items.question: What is the effect of H. pylori on parietal cell activity?
options: Has no effect on acid production
Inhibits H+ K+-ATPase activity leading to low acid production
items.explanation: H. pylori inhibits parietal cell H+, K+-ATPase activity through a Cag A-dependent mechanism, resulting in low acid production.
items.reference: Peptic Ulcer Disease and Related Disorders · PATHOPHYSIOLOGIC BASIS OF PUD (part 4)

items.question: Which immune cells are targeted by Vac A produced by H. pylori?
options: CD4 T cells, B cells, CD8 T cells, macrophages, and mast cells
items.explanation: Vac A targets human CD4 T cells, inhibiting their proliferation, and disrupts the normal function of B cells, CD8 T cells, macrophages, and mast cells.
items.reference: Peptic Ulcer Disease and Related Disorders · PATHOPHYSIOLOGIC BASIS OF PUD (part 4)

items.question: What role does urease play in H. pylori pathogenicity?
options: It helps the bacteria adhere to gastric epithelial cells.
It generates NH3, which can damage epithelial cells.
items.explanation: Urease allows H. pylori to reside in the acidic stomach by generating NH3, which can damage epithelial cells.
items.reference: Peptic Ulcer Disease and Related Disorders · PATHOPHYSIOLOGIC BASIS OF PUD (part 4)

items.question: Which gene is associated with the development of duodenal ulcers?
items.explanation: The DU-promoting gene A (dupA) is associated with the development of duodenal ulcers.

items.question: What effect does H. pylori infection have on gastric acid secretion after long-term infection?

items.question: What type of gastritis is associated with the formation of duodenal ulcers?

items.question: What is a common misconception regarding NSAID-induced pathology?

Concomitant use of anticoagulants increases risk.

items.question: Which mechanism is NOT involved in NSAID-induced mucosal injury?

items.question: Which statement about NSAIDs and peptic ulcer disease is true?

Aspirin does not cause GI complications at low doses.
NSAIDs do not affect prostaglandin synthesis.

items.question: Which of the following is a genetic factor implicated as a risk for peptic diathesis?

Cigarette-induced generation of noxious mucosal free radicals

items.question: What is the role of cigarette smoking in the pathogenesis of PUD?

It has been implicated in increasing ulcer diathesis.
It reduces the risk for H. pylori infection.

items.reference: Peptic Ulcer Disease and Related Disorders · NSAID-INDUCED DISEASE (part 2)

items.question: Which chronic disorder has a strong association with PUD?
items.explanation: Chronic renal failure is listed as having a strong association with PUD.

Tenderness to the right of the midline

items.question: Which of the following is NOT a method used to diagnose H. pylori?
items.explanation: A barium study is not used for diagnosing H. pylori.
items.reference: Peptic Ulcer Disease and Related Disorders · CLINICAL FEATURES (part 2)

items.question: Which combination of antacids is commonly used to avoid the side effects of constipation and loose stools?
options: A mixture of aluminum hydroxide and magnesium hydroxide

items.question: Which $ H_{2} $ receptor antagonist is known to have weak antiandrogenic side effects?
items.explanation: Cimetidine may cause weak antiandrogenic side effects such as reversible gynecomastia and impotence.
items.reference: Peptic Ulcer Disease and Related Disorders · Peptic Ulcer Disease | ACID-NEUTRALIZING/INHIBITORY DRUGS (part 1)

items.question: Which of the following is a rare adverse effect associated with $ H_{2} $ receptor antagonists?
items.explanation: $ H_{2} $ receptor antagonists can rarely cause pancytopenia, neutropenia, anemia, and thrombocytopenia.
items.reference: Peptic Ulcer Disease and Related Disorders · Peptic Ulcer Disease | ACID-NEUTRALIZING/INHIBITORY DRUGS (part 1)

items.question: Which PPI is the S-enantiomer of omeprazole?
items.explanation: Esomeprazole is identified as the S-enantiomer of omeprazole.
items.reference: Peptic Ulcer Disease and Related Disorders · ACID-NEUTRALIZING/INHIBITORY DRUGS (part 2)

items.question: Which PPI has a dual delayed-release system aimed at improving GERD treatment?
items.explanation: Dexlansoprazole is noted for its dual delayed-release system to improve GERD treatment.
items.reference: Peptic Ulcer Disease and Related Disorders · ACID-NEUTRALIZING/INHIBITORY DRUGS (part 2)

items.question: Which form of lansoprazole can be taken with or without water, beneficial for individuals with dysphagia?
items.explanation: Lansoprazole is available as an orally disintegrating tablet that can be taken with or without water.
items.reference: Peptic Ulcer Disease and Related Disorders · ACID-NEUTRALIZING/INHIBITORY DRUGS (part 2)

items.question: Which PPIs do not significantly interact with drugs metabolized by the cytochrome P450 system?
items.explanation: Rabeprazole, pantoprazole, and esomeprazole do not significantly interact with drugs metabolized by cytochrome P450.
items.reference: Peptic Ulcer Disease and Related Disorders · ACID-NEUTRALIZING/INHIBITORY DRUGS (part 2)

items.question: Which of the following infections has been associated with long-term PPI use according to a meta-analysis?
items.explanation: Long-term PPI use has been associated with an increased risk of community-acquired pneumonia.
items.reference: Peptic Ulcer Disease and Related Disorders · ACID-NEUTRALIZING/INHIBITORY DRUGS (part 3)

items.question: What is the hazard ratio for collagenous colitis development associated with PPI use, particularly lansoprazole?
items.explanation: The hazard ratio for collagenous colitis development associated with PPI use, particularly lansoprazole, is 4.5.
items.reference: Peptic Ulcer Disease and Related Disorders · ACID-NEUTRALIZING/INHIBITORY DRUGS (part 3)

items.question: What percentage increase in hypomagnesemia was found in PPI users compared to nonusers according to a meta-analysis?
items.explanation: A meta-analysis found a 40% increase in hypomagnesemia in PPI users compared to nonusers.
items.reference: Peptic Ulcer Disease and Related Disorders · ACID-NEUTRALIZING/INHIBITORY DRUGS (part 3)

items.question: What is the recommended action for patients taking clopidogrel with aspirin and other GI risk factors?
options: Switch to a different antiplatelet agent
items.explanation: Patients taking clopidogrel with aspirin and other GI risk factors should receive GI protective therapy.
items.reference: Peptic Ulcer Disease and Related Disorders · ACID-NEUTRALIZING/INHIBITORY DRUGS (part 3)

items.question: What is the recommended timing for administering PPI and clopidogrel to minimize competition with cytochrome P450?
options: Give both agents simultaneously in the morning.
Administer the PPI 30 min before breakfast and clopidogrel at bedtime.
Take both medications together at night.
PPI should be given after breakfast, and clopidogrel in the afternoon.
items.explanation: To minimize competition with cytochrome P450, it is recommended to give the PPI 30 min before breakfast and clopidogrel at bedtime.
items.reference: Peptic Ulcer Disease and Related Disorders · ACID-NEUTRALIZING/INHIBITORY DRUGS (part 4)

items.question: Which of the following is NOT a long-term side effect associated with PPI use?
items.explanation: Rebound hyperacidity is a concern upon abrupt withdrawal of PPIs, not a long-term side effect.
items.reference: Peptic Ulcer Disease and Related Disorders · ACID-NEUTRALIZING/INHIBITORY DRUGS (part 4)

items.question: Which novel agent has been approved by the FDA for use in the United States?
items.explanation: Vonoprazan has been approved by the FDA for use in the United States.
items.reference: Peptic Ulcer Disease and Related Disorders · ACID-NEUTRALIZING/INHIBITORY DRUGS (part 4)

items.question: Which cytoprotective agent is contraindicated in women who may be pregnant due to the risk of uterine bleeding and contractions?
items.explanation: Misoprostol, a prostaglandin analogue, is contraindicated in women who may be pregnant due to the risk of uterine bleeding and contractions.
items.reference: Peptic Ulcer Disease and Related Disorders · CYTOPROTECTIVE AGENTS

items.question: Which cytoprotective agent should be avoided in patients with chronic renal insufficiency?
items.explanation: Sucralfate should be avoided in patients with chronic renal insufficiency to prevent aluminum-induced neurotoxicity.
items.reference: Peptic Ulcer Disease and Related Disorders · CYTOPROTECTIVE AGENTS

items.question: Which cytoprotective agent is known for its effect against H. pylori?
items.explanation: Bismuth-containing preparations are used for their effect against H. pylori.
items.reference: Peptic Ulcer Disease and Related Disorders · CYTOPROTECTIVE AGENTS

items.question: What is the primary goal in treating peptic ulcer disease (PUD) related to H. pylori?
options: To provide relief of symptoms, promote ulcer healing, and prevent recurrence
items.explanation: The primary goal is to provide symptom relief, promote healing, and prevent recurrence.
items.reference: Peptic Ulcer Disease and Related Disorders · THERAPY OF H. PYLORI (part 1)

items.question: What percentage of ulcer recurrence is associated with documented eradication of H. pylori in PUD patients?
items.explanation: Eradication reduces ulcer recurrence to <10–20%.
items.reference: Peptic Ulcer Disease and Related Disorders · THERAPY OF H. PYLORI (part 1)

items.question: According to the Maastricht VI/Florence Consensus Report, when should a test-and-treat approach for H. pylori be considered?
options: If the local incidence of H. pylori is >20%
Only in patients over 60 years old
items.explanation: A test-and-treat approach is recommended if the local incidence of H. pylori is >20%.
items.reference: Peptic Ulcer Disease and Related Disorders · THERAPY OF H. PYLORI (part 1)

items.question: Which combination therapy duration provides the greatest efficacy in eradicating H. pylori?
items.explanation: A 14-day regimen is most effective for eradicating H. pylori.
items.reference: Peptic Ulcer Disease and Related Disorders · THERAPY OF H. PYLORI (part 1)

items.question: Which combination was the first triple regimen found effective against H. pylori?
items.explanation: The combination of bismuth, metronidazole, and tetracycline was the first effective triple regimen against H. pylori.
items.reference: Peptic Ulcer Disease and Related Disorders · THERAPY OF H. PYLORI (part 2)

items.question: What is a major concern when treating patients who may not need therapy for H. pylori?
items.explanation: Treating patients who may not need therapy can lead to the development of antibiotic-resistant H. pylori strains.
items.reference: Peptic Ulcer Disease and Related Disorders · THERAPY OF H. PYLORI (part 2)

items.question: What is a suggested first-line treatment in areas with high clarithromycin resistance?
items.explanation: In areas with high clarithromycin resistance, bismuth quadruple therapy is recommended as the first-line treatment.
items.reference: Peptic Ulcer Disease and Related Disorders · THERAPY OF H. PYLORI (part 2)

items.question: Which antibiotic has a reported resistance rate of <1% in the United States?
items.explanation: Resistance to amoxicillin is reported to be <1% in the United States.
items.reference: Peptic Ulcer Disease and Related Disorders · THERAPY OF H. PYLORI (part 2)

items.question: Which of the following factors are important in treatment failure for H. pylori eradication with triple therapy?
items.explanation: Treatment failure is often due to infection with a resistant organism.
items.reference: Peptic Ulcer Disease and Related Disorders · THERAPY OF H. PYLORI (part 3)

items.question: What is the recommended next step if eradication of H. pylori is not achieved after two courses of antibiotics?
options: Start a third course with the same antibiotics
items.explanation: Culture and sensitivity testing should be considered if eradication is not achieved after two courses.
items.reference: Peptic Ulcer Disease and Related Disorders · THERAPY OF H. PYLORI (part 3)

items.question: Which regimen has been shown to have an 86% cure rate for resistant strains of H. pylori?
items.explanation: The combination of PPI, amoxicillin, and rifabutin has an 86% cure rate for resistant strains.
items.reference: Peptic Ulcer Disease and Related Disorders · THERAPY OF H. PYLORI (part 3)

items.question: Which factor is associated with lower eradication rates of H. pylori?
items.explanation: The patient's country of origin, especially higher in Northeast Asia, is associated with lower eradication rates.
items.reference: Peptic Ulcer Disease and Related Disorders · THERAPY OF H. PYLORI (part 3)

items.subtopic: THERAPY OF NSAID-RELATED GASTRIC OR DUODENAL INJURY (part 1)
items.question: Which medication can heal gastric and duodenal ulcers regardless of whether NSAIDs are discontinued?
items.explanation: Only PPIs can heal gastric and duodenal ulcers independent of NSAID discontinuation.
items.reference: Peptic Ulcer Disease and Related Disorders · THERAPY OF NSAID-RELATED GASTRIC OR DUODENAL INJURY (part 1)

items.subtopic: THERAPY OF NSAID-RELATED GASTRIC OR DUODENAL INJURY (part 1)
items.question: Which COX-2 inhibitor was allowed to remain on the market after FDA review despite increased CV risk?
items.explanation: The FDA concluded that celecoxib is no less safe than several nonopioid pain killers and allowed it to remain on the market with a warning.
items.reference: Peptic Ulcer Disease and Related Disorders · THERAPY OF NSAID-RELATED GASTRIC OR DUODENAL INJURY (part 1)

items.subtopic: THERAPY OF NSAID-RELATED GASTRIC OR DUODENAL INJURY (part 1)
items.question: Which of the following is true about COX-2 inhibitors in relation to GI complications?
options: They are always superior to PPIs in preventing GI complications.
Their advantage in preventing GI complications can be offset by low-dose aspirin.
They have no risk of cardiovascular events.
They are recommended for all patients regardless of CV risk.
items.explanation: The CLASS study demonstrated that the advantage of celecoxib in preventing GI complications was offset when low-dose aspirin was used simultaneously.
items.reference: Peptic Ulcer Disease and Related Disorders · THERAPY OF NSAID-RELATED GASTRIC OR DUODENAL INJURY (part 1)

items.subtopic: THERAPY OF NSAID-RELATED GASTRIC OR DUODENAL INJURY (part 2)
items.question: Which group of individuals can receive nonselective NSAIDs without gastric protection?
options: Individuals with CV risk factors and high GI risk
Individuals not at risk for CV events, do not use aspirin, and are without risk for GI complications
Individuals requiring low-dose aspirin with moderate GI risk
Individuals with both CV and GI risks who require aspirin
items.explanation: Individuals not at risk for cardiovascular events, do not use aspirin, and have no gastrointestinal complications can take nonselective NSAIDs without gastric protection.
items.reference: Peptic Ulcer Disease and Related Disorders · THERAPY OF NSAID-RELATED GASTRIC OR DUODENAL INJURY (part 2)

items.subtopic: THERAPY OF NSAID-RELATED GASTRIC OR DUODENAL INJURY (part 2)
items.question: What is recommended for individuals with high potential risk for NSAID-induced GI toxicity but no CV risk factors?
options: Cautious use of a selective COX-2 inhibitor and co-therapy with high-dose PPI or misoprostol
items.explanation: For those at high risk for GI toxicity but no CV risk, a selective COX-2 inhibitor with co-therapy using high-dose PPI or misoprostol is recommended.
items.reference: Peptic Ulcer Disease and Related Disorders · THERAPY OF NSAID-RELATED GASTRIC OR DUODENAL INJURY (part 2)

items.subtopic: THERAPY OF NSAID-RELATED GASTRIC OR DUODENAL INJURY (part 2)
items.question: Which combination pill includes diclofenac and another agent?
items.explanation: A commercially available NSAID gastroprotective combination pill includes diclofenac and misoprostol.
items.reference: Peptic Ulcer Disease and Related Disorders · THERAPY OF NSAID-RELATED GASTRIC OR DUODENAL INJURY (part 2)

items.question: What is the recommended duration for triple or quadruple therapy when H. pylori is present in a patient with an ulcer?
items.explanation: Triple or quadruple therapy is recommended for 14 days.
items.reference: Peptic Ulcer Disease and Related Disorders · APPROACH AND THERAPY: SUMMARY (part 1)

items.question: Which test should be used to document H. pylori eradication after completing antibiotics?
items.explanation: The laboratory-based validated monoclonal stool antigen test or a urea breath test (UBT) should be used to document H. pylori eradication.
items.reference: Peptic Ulcer Disease and Related Disorders · APPROACH AND THERAPY: SUMMARY (part 1)

items.question: When should patients be off antisecretory agents before testing for H. pylori eradication?
items.explanation: Patients must be off antisecretory agents for at least 7 days when being tested for eradication of H. pylori.
items.reference: Peptic Ulcer Disease and Related Disorders · APPROACH AND THERAPY: SUMMARY (part 1)

items.question: What is the recommended action if a gastric ulcer fails to heal after 12 weeks?
items.explanation: A GU that fails to heal after 12 weeks should be considered refractory, and repeat endoscopy with biopsy is warranted.
items.reference: Peptic Ulcer Disease and Related Disorders · APPROACH AND THERAPY: SUMMARY (part 1)

items.question: Which patient group is more likely to present with atypical symptoms for a perforated peptic ulcer?
options: Elderly patients, obese individuals, and immunocompromised patients
items.explanation: Atypical presentation is more common in elderly patients (>70 years old), obese individuals, and immunocompromised patients.
items.reference: Peptic Ulcer Disease and Related Disorders · APPROACH AND THERAPY: SUMMARY (part 2)

items.question: What initial management steps are essential for a suspected perforated peptic ulcer?
options: Nasogastric suction, intravenous PPI, antibiotics, and surgical consultation
Oral intake of food and fluids
items.explanation: Early suspicion and intervention with nasogastric suction, intravenous PPI, antibiotics, and surgical consultation are essential.
items.reference: Peptic Ulcer Disease and Related Disorders · APPROACH AND THERAPY: SUMMARY (part 2)

items.question: What is the approximate recurrence rate of ulcers after highly selective vagotomy?
items.explanation: Ulcer recurrence rates are higher with highly selective vagotomy (≥10%), but the overall complication rates are the lowest.
items.reference: Peptic Ulcer Disease and Related Disorders · APPROACH AND THERAPY: SUMMARY (part 2)

items.question: Which surgical procedure has the highest ulcer recurrence rate?
items.explanation: The procedure that provides the lowest rates of ulcer recurrence (1%) but has the highest complication rate is vagotomy in combination with antrectomy.
items.reference: Peptic Ulcer Disease and Related Disorders · APPROACH AND THERAPY: SUMMARY (part 2)

items.question: What is the treatment of choice for an antral ulcer?
options: Antrectomy with a Billroth I anastomosis
items.explanation: Antrectomy with a Billroth I anastomosis is the treatment of choice for an antral ulcer.
items.reference: Peptic Ulcer Disease and Related Disorders · APPROACH AND THERAPY: SUMMARY (part 3)

items.question: What complication can occur after surgery due to incomplete drainage in afferent loop syndrome?
items.explanation: Incomplete drainage of bile and pancreatic secretions can lead to bacterial overgrowth in the afferent limb.
items.reference: Peptic Ulcer Disease and Related Disorders · APPROACH AND THERAPY: SUMMARY (part 3)

items.question: Which procedure is associated with ulcer recurrence rates approaching 30%?
items.explanation: Ulcer recurrence approaches 30% with the Kelling-Madlener procedure, which includes antectomy, intraoperative ulcer biopsy, and vagotomy.
items.reference: Peptic Ulcer Disease and Related Disorders · APPROACH AND THERAPY: SUMMARY (part 3)

items.question: What is a less aggressive surgical approach for fragile patients with high GU?
items.explanation: The Kelling-Madlener procedure, which includes antectomy, intraoperative ulcer biopsy, and vagotomy, is a less aggressive approach for fragile patients with high GU.
items.reference: Peptic Ulcer Disease and Related Disorders · APPROACH AND THERAPY: SUMMARY (part 3)

items.question: Which phase of dumping syndrome is associated with symptoms such as light-headedness, diaphoresis, and palpitations occurring 90 minutes to 3 hours after meals?
items.explanation: The late phase of dumping syndrome typically occurs 90 minutes to 3 hours after meals, characterized by vasomotor symptoms.
items.reference: Peptic Ulcer Disease and Related Disorders · APPROACH AND THERAPY: SUMMARY (part 4)

items.question: What is the primary dietary recommendation for managing early dumping syndrome?
options: Large meals rich in simple carbohydrates
Small, multiple meals devoid of simple carbohydrates
items.explanation: Dietary modification for dumping syndrome involves small, multiple meals devoid of simple carbohydrates.
items.reference: Peptic Ulcer Disease and Related Disorders · APPROACH AND THERAPY: SUMMARY (part 4)

items.question: Which medication is used subcutaneously in diet-refractory cases of dumping syndrome?
items.explanation: The somatostatin analogue octreotide is used subcutaneously in diet-refractory cases of dumping syndrome.
items.reference: Peptic Ulcer Disease and Related Disorders · APPROACH AND THERAPY: SUMMARY (part 4)

items.question: What surgical procedure may improve bowel frequency in patients with postvagotomy diarrhea?
options: Surgical reversal of a 10-cm segment of jejunum
items.explanation: Surgical reversal of a 10-cm segment of jejunum may yield improvement in bowel frequency for postvagotomy diarrhea.
items.reference: Peptic Ulcer Disease and Related Disorders · APPROACH AND THERAPY: SUMMARY (part 4)

items.question: Which vitamin deficiency is commonly seen in patients after a Billroth II gastrojejunostomy?
items.explanation: Malabsorption of vitamin D and calcium resulting in osteoporosis and osteomalacia is common after partial gastrectomy and gastrojejunostomy (Billroth II).
items.reference: Peptic Ulcer Disease and Related Disorders · APPROACH AND THERAPY: SUMMARY (part 5)

items.question: What complication can occur as a late consequence in up to 25% of post-partial gastrectomy patients?
items.explanation: Osteomalacia can occur as a late complication in up to 25% of post-partial gastrectomy patients.
items.reference: Peptic Ulcer Disease and Related Disorders · APPROACH AND THERAPY: SUMMARY (part 5)

items.question: What is the recommended treatment for bone abnormalities seen after partial gastrectomy?
items.explanation: The high incidence of these abnormalities in this subgroup of patients justifies treating them with vitamin D and calcium supplementation indefinitely.
items.reference: Peptic Ulcer Disease and Related Disorders · APPROACH AND THERAPY: SUMMARY (part 5)

items.question: Which type of tumors are most commonly associated with Zollinger-Ellison Syndrome?
items.explanation: Zollinger-Ellison Syndrome is defined by gastrinomas, which are well-differentiated neuroendocrine tumors.
items.reference: Peptic Ulcer Disease and Related Disorders · ZOLLINGER-ELLISON SYNDROME (part 1)

items.question: What percentage of gastrinomas are found within the 'gastrinoma triangle'?
items.explanation: Between 60 and 90% of gastrinomas are found within the hypothetical gastrinoma triangle.
items.reference: Peptic Ulcer Disease and Related Disorders · ZOLLINGER-ELLISON SYNDROME (part 1)

items.question: What is a common clinical manifestation in more than 90% of patients with Zollinger-Ellison Syndrome?
items.explanation: Peptic ulcer is the most common clinical manifestation, occurring in >90% of gastrinoma patients.
items.reference: Peptic Ulcer Disease and Related Disorders · ZOLLINGER-ELLISON SYNDROME (part 1)

items.question: Which factor has led to a decreased patient referral for gastrinoma evaluation?
options: Widespread availability and use of PPIs
items.explanation: The widespread availability and use of proton pump inhibitors (PPIs) have led to decreased patient referral for gastrinoma evaluation.
items.reference: Peptic Ulcer Disease and Related Disorders · ZOLLINGER-ELLISON SYNDROME (part 1)

items.question: Which gene mutation is responsible for MEN 1 syndrome?
items.explanation: The inactivating mutations of the MEN1 tumor suppressor gene cause MEN 1 syndrome.
items.reference: Peptic Ulcer Disease and Related Disorders · ZOLLINGER-ELLISON SYNDROME (part 2)

items.question: What percentage of patients with gastrinomas develop them as part of MEN 1 syndrome?
items.explanation: ~25% of patients with gastrinomas develop them in the presence of MEN 1 syndrome.
items.reference: Peptic Ulcer Disease and Related Disorders · ZOLLINGER-ELLISON SYNDROME (part 2)

items.question: Which endocrine organ is most commonly affected by tumors in MEN 1?
items.explanation: The parathyroid glands are the most commonly affected (80–90%) by tumors in MEN 1.
items.reference: Peptic Ulcer Disease and Related Disorders · ZOLLINGER-ELLISON SYNDROME (part 2)

items.explanation: Virtually all gastrinoma patients will have a gastrin level >150–200 pg/mL.

items.subtopic: TABLE 335-8 When to Obtain a Fasting Serum Gastrin Level (part 1)
items.question: Which of the following is NOT a cause of elevated fasting gastrin levels?
items.explanation: The passage does not list hyperthyroidism as a cause of elevated fasting gastrin levels.
items.reference: Peptic Ulcer Disease and Related Disorders · TABLE 335-8 When to Obtain a Fasting Serum Gastrin Level (part 1)

items.question: For how many days should a PPI be stopped before testing for fasting serum gastrin?
items.explanation: The effect of the PPI on gastrin levels and acid secretion will linger several days after stopping the PPI; therefore, it should be stopped for a minimum of 7 days before testing.

items.question: What is considered pathognomonic of ZES in terms of BAO?

items.question: Which imaging modality is considered superior for assessing tumor presence in patients with well-differentiated NETs such as gastrinomas?

items.question: What is the sensitivity rate of Endoscopic ultrasound (EUS) for finding duodenal lesions?
items.explanation: Endoscopic ultrasound (EUS) has a sensitivity rate of 43% for finding duodenal lesions.

items.subtopic: TABLE 335-8 When to Obtain a Fasting Serum Gastrin Level (part 2)
items.question: Which imaging study is particularly helpful in excluding small neoplasms within the pancreas and assessing surrounding lymph nodes?
items.explanation: Endoscopic ultrasound (EUS) is particularly helpful in excluding small neoplasms within the pancreas and assessing surrounding lymph nodes.
items.reference: Peptic Ulcer Disease and Related Disorders · TABLE 335-8 When to Obtain a Fasting Serum Gastrin Level (part 2)

items.question: What is the initial dose range of PPIs like omeprazole or esomeprazole for treating Zollinger-Ellison Syndrome?
items.explanation: The initial PPI dose should be in the range of 60 mg in divided doses.
items.reference: Peptic Ulcer Disease and Related Disorders · Zollinger-Ellison Syndrome (part 1)

items.question: What is a significant challenge in achieving definitive surgical cure for gastrinoma patients with MEN 1?
items.explanation: Surgical therapy remains controversial due to the difficulty in rendering these patients disease-free.
items.reference: Peptic Ulcer Disease and Related Disorders · Zollinger-Ellison Syndrome (part 1)

items.question: What is a potential benefit of using systemic therapy with radiolabeled somatostatin analogues for metastatic NETs?
options: Substantial improvement in symptoms and progression-free survival
items.explanation: Radiolabeled somatostatin analogues show promise in terms of radiographic regression, symptoms, and progression-free survival.
items.reference: Peptic Ulcer Disease and Related Disorders · Zollinger-Ellison Syndrome (part 1)

items.question: Why might early surgery be deferred for MEN 1 patients with ZES?
options: Tumors are typically benign and rarely lead to mortality
items.explanation: The clinical course tends to be benign, rarely leading to disease-related mortality.
items.reference: Peptic Ulcer Disease and Related Disorders · Zollinger-Ellison Syndrome (part 1)

items.question: Which of the following factors is associated with a poor prognosis in patients with gastrinoma?
items.explanation: Higher gastrin levels (>10,000 pg/mL) are associated with a poor prognosis in patients with gastrinoma.

items.question: Which statement best describes the term 'gastritis' as used in medical literature?
options: Gastritis is synonymous with mucosal erythema seen during endoscopy.
Gastritis refers to histologically documented inflammation of the gastric mucosa.
Gastritis and dyspepsia are interchangeable terms.
Gastritis always presents with typical clinical manifestations.

items.question: What is a common cause of acute gastritis?
items.explanation: Acute infection with H. pylori is a common cause of acute gastritis.

items.question: What histologic finding is typically seen in acute H. pylori gastritis?
options: Marked infiltrate of neutrophils with edema and hyperemia.

items.question: Which type of chronic gastritis is predominantly associated with H. pylori infection?
items.explanation: Type B chronic gastritis is associated with H. pylori infection and predominantly affects the antrum.

items.question: Which organism is NOT typically involved in acute infectious gastritides?

items.question: Which type of gastrin is associated with achlorhydria and can lead to hypergastrinemia?

items.reference: Peptic Ulcer Disease and Related Disorders · GASTRIC PHYSIOLOGY (part 2)

items.assertion: Histamine stimulates gastric acid secretion indirectly by inhibiting somatostatin release.
items.reason: Histamine activates the histamine $H_{3}$ receptor on D cells, which inhibits somatostatin release.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the assertion.
items.reference: Peptic Ulcer Disease and Related Disorders · GASTRIC PHYSIOLOGY (part 3)

items.assertion: The cephalic phase of gastric acid secretion is triggered by nutrients entering the stomach.
items.reason: Sight, smell, and taste of food stimulate the vagus nerve during the cephalic phase.
items.explanation: The assertion is false; the cephalic phase is triggered by sight, smell, and taste, not nutrients entering the stomach.
items.reference: Peptic Ulcer Disease and Related Disorders · GASTRIC PHYSIOLOGY (part 3)

items.subtopic: GASTRIC PHYSIOLOGY (part 4) | PATHOPHYSIOLOGIC BASIS OF PUD (part 1)
items.assertion: The majority of gastric ulcers are associated with H. pylori or NSAID-induced mucosal damage.
items.reason: H. pylori and NSAIDs contribute significantly to the pathogenesis of peptic ulcer disease.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason is a correct explanation for the assertion.
items.reference: Peptic Ulcer Disease and Related Disorders · GASTRIC PHYSIOLOGY (part 4) | PATHOPHYSIOLOGIC BASIS OF PUD (part 1)

items.subtopic: GASTRIC PHYSIOLOGY (part 4) | PATHOPHYSIOLOGIC BASIS OF PUD (part 1)
items.assertion: Duodenal ulcers often occur in the third portion of the duodenum.
items.reason: The majority of duodenal ulcers are located within 3 cm of the pylorus.
items.explanation: The assertion is false because most duodenal ulcers occur in the first portion, not the third.
items.reference: Peptic Ulcer Disease and Related Disorders · GASTRIC PHYSIOLOGY (part 4) | PATHOPHYSIOLOGIC BASIS OF PUD (part 1)

items.assertion: The prevalence of H. pylori infection is higher in developing countries.
items.reason: In developing parts of the world, up to 80% may be infected by age 20 due to factors like poor sanitation and crowded living conditions.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the assertion.
items.reference: Peptic Ulcer Disease and Related Disorders · PATHOPHYSIOLOGIC BASIS OF PUD (part 3)

items.assertion: All individuals with H. pylori infection develop peptic ulcer disease.
items.reason: Only a small percentage of infected individuals actually develop ulcers, due to host and bacterial factors.
items.explanation: The assertion is false; not all individuals with H. pylori develop peptic ulcer disease.
items.reference: Peptic Ulcer Disease and Related Disorders · PATHOPHYSIOLOGIC BASIS OF PUD (part 3)

items.assertion: H. pylori infection leads to increased epithelial cell turnover.
items.reason: This is due to activated neutrophil-mediated production of reactive oxygen or nitrogen species.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, but the reason does not directly explain the increased epithelial cell turnover.
items.reference: Peptic Ulcer Disease and Related Disorders · PATHOPHYSIOLOGIC BASIS OF PUD (part 4)

items.assertion: Cag A contributes to the development of gastric cancer.
items.reason: This is due to its interaction with oncoprotein Cag A and formation of 8-nitroguanine.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains how Cag A contributes to gastric cancer development.
items.reference: Peptic Ulcer Disease and Related Disorders · PATHOPHYSIOLOGIC BASIS OF PUD (part 4)

items.assertion: Eradication of H. pylori reduces the likelihood of GI complications in high-risk individuals.
items.reason: H. pylori and NSAIDs are independent and synergistic risk factors for PUD.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the assertion.
items.reference: Peptic Ulcer Disease and Related Disorders · NSAID-INDUCED DISEASE (part 1)

items.assertion: SSRIs decrease the risk of NSAID-induced GI bleeding.
items.reason: SSRIs increase serotonin content in platelets, enhancing platelet aggregation.
items.explanation: The assertion is false; SSRIs actually have a synergistic effect on inducing GI bleeding by decreasing serotonin content.
items.reference: Peptic Ulcer Disease and Related Disorders · NSAID-INDUCED DISEASE (part 1)

items.assertion: The incidence of perforation in the elderly is increasing.
items.reason: This increase is due to the higher use of NSAIDs in this population.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the assertion.
items.reference: Peptic Ulcer Disease and Related Disorders · CLINICAL FEATURES (part 1)

items.assertion: Pain that awakes a patient from sleep is specific only to duodenal ulcers.
items.reason: This symptom is also present in one-third of patients with non-ulcer dyspepsia.
items.explanation: The assertion is true, but the reason is false as it contradicts the specificity implied by the assertion.
items.reference: Peptic Ulcer Disease and Related Disorders · CLINICAL FEATURES (part 1)

items.assertion: Gastric outlet obstruction is the most common ulcer-related complication.
items.reason: Gastric outlet obstruction occurs in 1–2% of patients with ulcers.
items.explanation: The assertion is false; gastric outlet obstruction is the least common ulcer-related complication.
items.reference: Peptic Ulcer Disease and Related Disorders · CLINICAL FEATURES (part 2)

items.assertion: Barium studies are commonly used as a first test for documenting an ulcer.
items.reason: Endoscopy provides more sensitive and specific examination of the upper GI tract.
items.explanation: The assertion is false; barium studies are rarely used as a first test.
items.reference: Peptic Ulcer Disease and Related Disorders · CLINICAL FEATURES (part 2)

items.assertion: Famotidine is a more potent $ H_{2} $ receptor antagonist than cimetidine.
items.reason: Famotidine can be used once daily for ulcer prevention, unlike other $ H_{2} $ blockers.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true; however, the reason does not explain why famotidine is more potent.
items.reference: Peptic Ulcer Disease and Related Disorders · Peptic Ulcer Disease | ACID-NEUTRALIZING/INHIBITORY DRUGS (part 1)

items.assertion: PPIs are most effective when administered before a meal.
items.reason: The efficacy of PPIs is maximized because the pumps need to be activated for these agents to be effective.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, with the reason correctly explaining why PPIs should be taken before meals.
items.reference: Peptic Ulcer Disease and Related Disorders · ACID-NEUTRALIZING/INHIBITORY DRUGS (part 2)

items.assertion: Long-term use of PPIs has been definitively linked to the development of gastric carcinoid tumors in humans.
items.reason: Carcinoid tumors developed in some animals given these drugs chronically.
items.explanation: The assertion is false as extensive human experience does not show gastric carcinoid tumor development, despite animal studies.
items.reference: Peptic Ulcer Disease and Related Disorders · ACID-NEUTRALIZING/INHIBITORY DRUGS (part 2)

items.assertion: PPIs may increase the risk of hip fractures in older women.
items.reason: Long-term use of PPIs is associated with an observed increase in hip fractures, although the absolute risk remains low.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, but the reason does not fully explain the assertion as the mechanism is unclear.
items.reference: Peptic Ulcer Disease and Related Disorders · ACID-NEUTRALIZING/INHIBITORY DRUGS (part 3)

items.assertion: PPIs compete with clopidogrel for metabolism by cytochrome P450 (CYP2C19).
items.reason: This competition may reduce the antiplatelet effect of clopidogrel.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason is the correct explanation for the assertion.
items.reference: Peptic Ulcer Disease and Related Disorders · ACID-NEUTRALIZING/INHIBITORY DRUGS (part 3)

items.assertion: Tenatoprazole may be beneficial for inhibiting nocturnal acid secretion.
items.reason: It contains an imidazopyridine ring which promotes irreversible proton pump inhibition and has a longer half-life than other PPIs.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason is the correct explanation of the assertion.
items.reference: Peptic Ulcer Disease and Related Disorders · ACID-NEUTRALIZING/INHIBITORY DRUGS (part 4)

items.assertion: H. pylori eradication should be considered in first-degree relatives of family members with gastric cancer.
items.reason: The Maastricht VI/Florence Consensus Report recommends this due to the risk factors associated with gastric cancer.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the assertion.
items.reference: Peptic Ulcer Disease and Related Disorders · THERAPY OF H. PYLORI (part 1)

items.assertion: A single antibiotic is effective in eradicating H. pylori.
items.reason: Combination therapy for 14 days provides the greatest efficacy.
items.explanation: The assertion is false; no single agent is effective, but combination therapy is.
items.reference: Peptic Ulcer Disease and Related Disorders · THERAPY OF H. PYLORI (part 1)

items.assertion: The addition of acid suppression to H. pylori treatment regimens assists in bacterial eradication.
items.reason: Acid suppression provides early symptom relief and enhances bacterial eradication.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the assertion.
items.reference: Peptic Ulcer Disease and Related Disorders · THERAPY OF H. PYLORI (part 2)

items.assertion: Resistance to tetracycline is common in H. pylori strains.
items.reason: Resistance to tetracycline and rifabutin is reported to be <2% in the United States.
items.explanation: The assertion is false because resistance to tetracycline is not common, as indicated by the low percentage.
items.reference: Peptic Ulcer Disease and Related Disorders · THERAPY OF H. PYLORI (part 2)

items.assertion: Probiotics have been shown to increase eradication rates of H. pylori.
items.reason: Certain probiotics like Lactobacillus spp., Saccharomyces spp., Bifidobacterium spp., and Bacillus clausii did not alter eradication rates but decreased antibiotic-associated side effects.
items.explanation: Probiotics do not increase eradication rates; they decrease side effects.
items.reference: Peptic Ulcer Disease and Related Disorders · THERAPY OF H. PYLORI (part 3)

items.assertion: The use of N-acetylcysteine as a mucolytic agent is confirmed to improve H. pylori eradication.
items.reason: N-acetylcysteine has been examined for its potential to destroy the H. pylori biofilm and impair antibiotic resistance, but more studies are needed.
items.explanation: The use of N-acetylcysteine is not yet confirmed; further studies are required.
items.reference: Peptic Ulcer Disease and Related Disorders · THERAPY OF H. PYLORI (part 3)

items.subtopic: THERAPY OF NSAID-RELATED GASTRIC OR DUODENAL INJURY (part 1)
items.assertion: COX-2 inhibitors are associated with increased risk of heart attacks and strokes.
items.reason: The FDA concluded that celecoxib is no less safe than several nonopioid pain killers but has an increased CV risk.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the association with increased cardiovascular risks.
items.reference: Peptic Ulcer Disease and Related Disorders · THERAPY OF NSAID-RELATED GASTRIC OR DUODENAL INJURY (part 1)

items.assertion: Hemorrhage is the most common ulcer-related complication.
items.reason: It occurs in ~15–25% of patients with ulcers.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains why hemorrhage is considered the most common complication.
items.reference: Peptic Ulcer Disease and Related Disorders · APPROACH AND THERAPY: SUMMARY (part 1)

items.assertion: Patients with complicated ulcer disease should be treated with long-term acid suppression.
items.reason: This makes documentation of H. pylori eradication unnecessary in some cases.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains why long-term acid suppression might make H. pylori eradication documentation moot.
items.reference: Peptic Ulcer Disease and Related Disorders · APPROACH AND THERAPY: SUMMARY (part 1)

items.assertion: Highly selective vagotomy is preferred over truncal vagotomy for reducing complications.
items.reason: Highly selective vagotomy has the lowest overall complication rates among surgical procedures for duodenal ulcers.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains why highly selective vagotomy is preferred.
items.reference: Peptic Ulcer Disease and Related Disorders · APPROACH AND THERAPY: SUMMARY (part 2)

items.assertion: Laparoscopic surgery for PUD has been shown to increase postoperative pain.
items.reason: Laparoscopic repair of perforated peptic ulcers is associated with decreased postoperative pain compared to open surgery.
items.explanation: The assertion is false, but the reason is true; laparoscopic surgery actually decreases postoperative pain.
items.reference: Peptic Ulcer Disease and Related Disorders · APPROACH AND THERAPY: SUMMARY (part 2)

items.assertion: Epigastric abdominal pain is the most frequent presenting complaint after recurrent ulcers post-surgery.
items.reason: Ulcer recurrence tends to be more progressive in severity and duration than observed with duodenal ulcers before surgery.
items.explanation: Both statements are true, but the reason does not explain why epigastric abdominal pain is the most frequent complaint.
items.reference: Peptic Ulcer Disease and Related Disorders · APPROACH AND THERAPY: SUMMARY (part 3)

items.assertion: Incomplete vagotomy can be ruled out by gastric acid analysis coupled with sham feeding.
items.reason: An increase in gastric acid output during sham feeding indicates an intact vagus nerve.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains how incomplete vagotomy can be ruled out.
items.reference: Peptic Ulcer Disease and Related Disorders · APPROACH AND THERAPY: SUMMARY (part 3)

items.assertion: The late phase of dumping syndrome results from hypoglycemia.
items.reason: Hypoglycemia is caused by excessive insulin release after meals rich in simple carbohydrates.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, with the reason correctly explaining the late phase of dumping syndrome.
items.reference: Peptic Ulcer Disease and Related Disorders · APPROACH AND THERAPY: SUMMARY (part 4)

items.assertion: Mild steatorrhea after partial gastric resection is due to decreased pancreatic secretory response.
items.reason: Decreased gastric acid production contributes to maldigestion and malabsorption.
items.explanation: Both statements are true, but the reason does not explain mild steatorrhea; it's due to multiple factors including decreased pancreatic secretory response.
items.reference: Peptic Ulcer Disease and Related Disorders · APPROACH AND THERAPY: SUMMARY (part 4)

items.assertion: Osteomalacia occurs as a late complication in post-partial gastrectomy patients.
items.reason: This is due to malabsorption of vitamin D and calcium after the surgery.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains why osteomalacia occurs.
items.reference: Peptic Ulcer Disease and Related Disorders · APPROACH AND THERAPY: SUMMARY (part 5)

items.assertion: Gastrin stimulates acid secretion through gastrin receptors on parietal cells.
items.reason: Gastrin also induces histamine release from ECL cells, contributing to acid secretion.
items.explanation: Both statements are true, but the reason is not the correct explanation for the assertion.
items.reference: Peptic Ulcer Disease and Related Disorders · ZOLLINGER-ELLISON SYNDROME (part 1)

items.assertion: Diarrhea in Zollinger-Ellison Syndrome patients may occur independent of an ulcer.
items.reason: The diarrhea is solely due to peptic ulcers and not related to other factors.
items.explanation: The assertion is true, but the reason is false as diarrhea can also be multifactorial.
items.reference: Peptic Ulcer Disease and Related Disorders · ZOLLINGER-ELLISON SYNDROME (part 1)

items.subtopic: TABLE 335-8 When to Obtain a Fasting Serum Gastrin Level (part 1)
items.assertion: A secretin stimulation test is highly sensitive and specific for diagnosing ZES.
items.reason: An increase in gastrin of ≥120 pg within 15 min of secretin injection has a sensitivity and specificity of >90% for ZES.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason is the correct explanation of the assertion.
items.reference: Peptic Ulcer Disease and Related Disorders · TABLE 335-8 When to Obtain a Fasting Serum Gastrin Level (part 1)

items.subtopic: TABLE 335-8 When to Obtain a Fasting Serum Gastrin Level (part 1)
items.assertion: A pH >3 obtained during gastric fluid measurement excludes ZES.
items.reason: A pH <3 is suggestive of a gastrinoma, but a pH >3 is not helpful in excluding the diagnosis.
items.explanation: The assertion is false because a pH >3 does not exclude ZES; the reason correctly states that a pH >3 is not helpful in excluding the diagnosis.
items.reference: Peptic Ulcer Disease and Related Disorders · TABLE 335-8 When to Obtain a Fasting Serum Gastrin Level (part 1)

items.assertion: PPIs are the treatment of choice for Zollinger-Ellison Syndrome.
items.reason: They reduce parietal cell activity to a greater degree than somatostatin analogues.
items.explanation: Both A and R are true, and R is the correct explanation of A.
items.reference: Peptic Ulcer Disease and Related Disorders · Zollinger-Ellison Syndrome (part 1)

items.assertion: Surgical therapy for gastrinoma in MEN 1 patients often results in disease-free status.
items.reason: The clinical course of MEN 1 patients tends to be benign and rarely leads to mortality.
items.explanation: A is false, but R is true; surgery does not often result in disease-free status.
items.reference: Peptic Ulcer Disease and Related Disorders · Zollinger-Ellison Syndrome (part 1)

items.assertion: PPIs are the treatment of choice for stress prophylaxis.
items.reason: Meta-analysis shows PPIs' superiority in preventing stress-associated GI bleeding without increasing infection risk or mortality.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason is the correct explanation.
items.reference: Peptic Ulcer Disease and Related Disorders · Zollinger-Ellison Syndrome (part 2) | STRESS-RELATED MUCOSAL INJURY

items.assertion: Vagotomy and antrectomy are preferred surgical options for stress ulcer/gastritis.
items.reason: Total gastrectomy has an exceedingly high mortality rate in this setting.
items.explanation: The assertion is false; total gastrectomy, not vagotomy or antrectomy, should be considered if surgery is necessary.
items.reference: Peptic Ulcer Disease and Related Disorders · Zollinger-Ellison Syndrome (part 2) | STRESS-RELATED MUCOSAL INJURY

items.assertion: Acute gastritis can evolve into chronic gastritis if not treated.
items.reason: H. pylori infection causes acute gastritis which, if untreated, progresses to chronic gastritis.
items.explanation: If not treated, the picture of acute H. pylori gastritis will evolve into one of chronic gastritis.
items.reference: Peptic Ulcer Disease and Related Disorders · GASTRITIS

items.assertion: Pernicious anemia is associated with Type A gastritis.
items.reason: Type A gastritis involves antibodies against parietal cells and intrinsic factor.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the presence of these antibodies explains the association with pernicious anemia.
items.reference: Peptic Ulcer Disease and Related Disorders · TABLE 335-10 Classification of Gastritis (part 1)

items.assertion: Herpetic gastritis is commonly seen in immunocompetent individuals.
items.reason: Herpetic gastritis occurs more frequently in immunocompromised patients such as those with AIDS.
items.explanation: The assertion is false because herpetic gastritis is more common in immunocompromised individuals, not immunocompetent ones.
items.reference: Peptic Ulcer Disease and Related Disorders · TABLE 335-10 Classification of Gastritis (part 1)

items.assertion: H. pylori infection is associated with a higher incidence of gastric adenocarcinoma.
items.reason: Seropositivity for H. pylori increases the risk of gastric cancer by three to nine times.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the association.
items.reference: Peptic Ulcer Disease and Related Disorders · TABLE 335-10 Classification of Gastritis (part 2)

items.assertion: H. pylori eradication is currently recommended as a general preventative measure for gastric cancer.
items.reason: Eradication of H. pylori has been shown to reduce the risk of developing gastric adenocarcinoma.
items.explanation: The assertion is false; while eradication reduces risk, it is not yet recommended as a general preventative measure.
items.reference: Peptic Ulcer Disease and Related Disorders · TABLE 335-10 Classification of Gastritis (part 2)

items.assertion: Eradication of H. pylori is often recommended even if PUD or a low-grade MALT lymphoma is not present.
items.reason: H. pylori eradication can prevent the development of complications associated with chronic gastritis.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, but the reason does not directly explain why H. pylori eradication is recommended in all cases.
items.reference: Peptic Ulcer Disease and Related Disorders · Chronic Gastritis

items.assertion: Therapy with glucocorticoids or sodium cromoglycate has obtained unclear results for lymphocytic gastritis.
items.reason: H. pylori plays a significant role in the pathogenesis of lymphocytic gastritis.
items.explanation: The assertion is true, but the reason is false because H. pylori probably plays no significant role in lymphocytic gastritis.
items.reference: Peptic Ulcer Disease and Related Disorders · Chronic Gastritis

items.assertion: Ménétrier's disease is considered a form of gastritis.
items.reason: The lamina propria in MD may contain a mild chronic inflammatory infiltrate including eosinophils and plasma cells.
items.explanation: Despite the presence of inflammation, Ménétrier's disease is not classified as a form of gastritis.
items.reference: Peptic Ulcer Disease and Related Disorders · MÉNÉTRIER'S DISEASE | Ménétrier's Disease

items.assertion: Cetuximab has been shown to induce histologic remission in some patients with Ménétrier's disease.
items.reason: Cetuximab inhibits the overstimulation of the EGFR pathway, which is associated with MD.
items.explanation: The inhibition of the EGFR pathway by cetuximab leads to histologic remission in Ménétrier's disease.
items.reference: Peptic Ulcer Disease and Related Disorders · MÉNÉTRIER'S DISEASE | Ménétrier's Disease
